Tafseer:
Ta-Ha.

This is among the abbreviated letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at). A detailed discussion on these letters can be found at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah.

"We have not sent down to you the Qur'an to cause you distress."

**The Hard Labor of the Prophet (peace be upon him) in Conveing the Message, and His Loving Reassurance:**

In an exceptionally affectionate and loving manner, Allah Almighty reassures the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that the massive responsibility he has taken upon himself regarding the propagation and calling to the Qur'an, and the grueling labor he is enduring for its sake, is by no means demanded of him. His responsibility is only to convey this Book to the people. Those who possess the capacity to recognize truth and retain a spark of the fear of Allah will accept it. It is not his responsibility to force it into the hearts of those who are entirely devoid of these capacities, to the point of making his own life miserable over them.

We have clarified in various places in this book that despite working day and night in the mission of calling to Allah, when the Prophet (peace be upon him) saw people denying such an obvious truth and saw no signs of their hearts softening, he would worry that perhaps some shortcoming on his own part was responsible. Out of this feeling, his activities in the mission of calling would increase even further, and despite exhausting all his efforts, he would find no peace of mind. Regarding this very state of his, Allah Almighty declared that this Qur'an was not sent down to you (peace be upon him) so that your life should be made miserable. Discharge only the responsibility that is yours; the responsibility for the faith and Islam of others does not rest upon you.

"But only as a reminder for those who fear [Allah]."

**The Qur'an is a Reminder:**

This phrase clarifies the true nature of the Qur'an, defines the limits of the Prophet's responsibility, and identifies who will be influenced by it and who will remain deprived.

It is stated that this is only a "tadhkirah", which means a reminder. Obviously, a reminder is not given for something brought from the outside; rather, it is for something that the addressee already possesses but has forgotten. If one reflects on the Qur'an, its primary nature is precisely this. It is not some foreign or alien thing. It does not impose an external concept upon us; rather, it reminds us of those very truths that already exist within our own nature but from which we are heedless. The arguments the Qur'an uses to remind us of these truths are also those very arguments of the horizons and souls that already exist in the treasury of our intellect. However, we either do not use them or avoid accepting their self-evident conclusions. The history the Qur'an presents for lesson and moral admonition is also the history of those nations that were well-known to the target audience. The teachings of the prophets (peace be upon them) it references are of those very prophets whose descendants and followers the people claimed to be.

**The Condition to be Influenced by the Qur'an:**

The attribute of those who will be influenced by this reminder is described as "liman yakhsha" (for those who fear). Contextual clues show that the object of "yakhsha" is omitted here. In the light of parallels in the Qur'an, if we supply this omitted object, the complete statement would be: "liman yakhsha Rabbahu bil-ghayb" — meaning, for those who fear their Lord in the unseen. In other words, this is the same truth expressed in the second verse of Surah Al-Baqarah as "hudan lil-muttaqeen" (a guidance for the godfearing). We explained in detail there that this piety and fear exist within all those whose nature has been preserved from corruption. If such people fall into heedlessness, they eventually wake up through the Qur'an's reminder. As for those who corrupt their own nature through their evil deeds, no matter how much you shake them, they will not turn. No logic works on them except the logic of the rod, like donkeys. They want to believe only after seeing everything with their physical eyes, but before God, the only belief that is credible is that which is accepted by seeing with the eyes of the intellect and the heart, not the physical eyes.

**The Prophet is a Reminder, Not a Controller:**

From the Qur'an being a reminder, it also follows that the status of the Prophet (peace be upon him) is that of a "mudhakkir" (reminder-giver), not a "musaytir" (controller). Your duty was only to remind people of their forgotten lesson. Whether people remember this lesson or not was their responsibility, not yours. The Qur'an has clarified this reality here so that if the people of your nation are not benefiting from your reminding, you should not distress yourself; they are answerable for it before Allah, not you.

"A revelation from Him Who created the earth and the highest heavens."

**The Concept of "Tanzeel" (Revelation):**

We have explained the meaning of "tanzeel" in another place as sending down something with meticulous care, order, and in a gradual manner. The meaning is that since this Qur'an has been sent down for the reminding of people, Allah did not content Himself with merely letting the message reach the people somehow once; rather, He sent it down with great care, gradually, and in intervals, so that the people would have no excuse left that there was any deficiency in conveying or explaining the matter.

**The Qur'an is Not the Request of a Petitioner, But the Command of the Creator:**

"From Him Who created the earth and the highest heavens" — this highlights the grandeur of the Qur'an: that it has been sent down by the Creator and Master of the earth and the high heavens. It is not an idle word to be blown away in the wind, nor is it the request of a petitioner which, if accepted by the people, would be a favor to the petitioner, and if rejected, would leave him deprived. Rather, it is the binding command of the Creator of the earth and the heavens; if people reject it, they should think carefully about what the consequences could be.

In this, there is also an indication for the Prophet (peace be upon him) that he should present it before the people in the exact manner that the word of the Creator and Master of the heavens and earth should be presented. For this, there is no need for excessive pleading, nor any importunity or insistence. In accepting it, the benefit is the people's own, not God's. This is not a pleading request; rather, it is the scripture of guidance for creation. Those who reject it only bring about their own ruin. They will not harm you in the least.

This subject has been expressed in various styles in different places in the Qur'an. In Surah 'Abasa, this reality is clarified as: "No indeed! This is a reminder. So let whoever wills pay heed to it. It is written on honored sheets, exalted and purified, by the hands of scribes, noble and dutiful." (80:11-16)

"The Most Merciful [Who] above the Throne established Himself. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth and whatever is between them and whatever is under the soil."

**The Grandeur of the Qur'an and Reference to the Divine Attributes it Manifests:**

This is both a statement of the grandeur of the Qur'an and an indication of those attributes of Allah of which it is a manifestation. It is stated that the Creator of the earth and the heavens is Al-Rahman (the Most Merciful). The demand of His mercy is that He does not merely create the creation and leave it to itself; rather, just as He provided the physical means and resources for its material life, He also arranged for its spiritual guidance. Consequently, for the guidance of human, He blessed him with intellect and speech, and added to this favor by sending down His Book for his guidance. This reality was referenced in Surah Al-Rahman as: "The Most Merciful, taught the Qur'an." The meaning is that those who are rejecting the Qur'an today are harming neither God nor His Messenger, but are depriving themselves of God's greatest mercy.

**"Above the Throne established Himself":** This describes the attribute of the Lord of Mercy: that He did not create the world and then sit apart in some corner; rather, He is actually established upon the Throne of governance over His universe. The true Ruler of this universe is He alone, which is why He has informed people of His commands and laws through the Qur'an. After this information, those who act according to their own desires will see their end on the Day of Account.

**"To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth and whatever is between them and whatever is under the soil":** This cuts down the root of every seed of shirk. It is stated that the Creator and Master of the heavens, the earth, everything between them, and everything under the earth is He alone. He created all of them, and His authority is over all of them. No corner of this universe is outside His sovereignty. Whatever freedom has been granted to anyone in any specific domain is also under His command and decree. For this reason, let no one assume that they can escape from God anywhere, or that anyone else can grant them refuge.

"And if you speak aloud, then indeed, He knows the secret and what is even more hidden."

**A Style of Arabic Expression:**

In accordance with the style of eloquent Arabic, the contrasting words are omitted here based on contextual clues. We have explained this style in various places. If we expand this omission, the complete statement would be: "And if you speak aloud, or if you whisper, then indeed He knows what is spoken aloud, what is secret, and what is even more hidden." That is, whether you speak openly or in secret, God knows the open, the hidden, and the most hidden.

**Consolation to the Prophet (peace be upon him) from a New Angle:**

The Prophet (peace be upon him) is consoled from another perspective: he should rest assured that whatever he says to his Lord in his difficulties, and whatever prayer or supplication he makes, whether secretly or openly, and whatever worry he experiences, whether it is expressed by the tongue or remains in the hidden corners of the heart, his All-Knowing Lord is aware of everything. The purpose of expressing this reality is obvious: its essential corollary is that when your Lord is aware of even the most hidden secret, He will make every difficulty of yours easy and guide you at every turn. If there is a delay in any matter, it is based on some wisdom and benefit, and in it lies goodness and blessing for you. If further details are desired, please refer to our commentary under Surah Maryam, verse 64: "And your Lord is never forgetful."

"Allah - there is no deity except Him. To Him belong the best names."

**Instruction of Entrustment (Tafweedh):**

This is an instruction to the Prophet (peace be upon him) for complete entrustment of his affairs to Allah: "Instead of worrying over the attitude of the opponents and exhausting yourself over them, entrust your matter entirely to your Lord. There is no deity except Allah, and in His presence, you are not dependent on anyone else."

**A Treasury of Solace and Peace:**

"To Him belong the best names" — this phrase is a treasury of solace and peace. In the preceding verses, the solace given to the Prophet (peace be upon him) was in the light of the demands of Allah's attributes—His grandeur, His mercy, His wisdom, His uniqueness, and His knowledge. But God's attributes are not limited to these! He is characterized by all good and high attributes, so obviously, the demands of these attributes will also manifest for the Prophet (peace be upon him) according to the circumstances. In this phrase, the Prophet (peace be upon him) is given the glad tiding that all good attributes belong to God alone and they all shade you; whichever of them you seek support from, you will benefit from its blessings. Other than them, you are not dependent on any other support.

There is consensus that "names" (Asma) here refers to the divine attributes, because all of God's names are expressions of His attributes.

### Subsequent Subject Matter: Verses 9 to 98

In the preceding verses, the solace and reassurance given to the Prophet (peace be upon him) was in the light of Allah's names and attributes. Now, the story of Moses (peace be upon him) is presented, in which those very truths that were expressed above in a conceptual manner are shown in active, practical life. The trials Moses (peace be upon him) had to pass through before and after his mission, and the manner in which Allah's help supported him, are described in detail in this story. A massive portion of the phases the Holy Prophet (peace be upon him) was to face in the future was still behind the veil; this story placed those phases before him and indicated the preparations required for them. Practical examples influence human life far more than anything else. Specifically, the story of one prophet is, for another prophet, nothing less than his own personal biography.

Recite the subsequent verses in this light:

"And has the story of Moses reached you?"

**The Special Significance of Moses's Story for This Ummah:**

This style of expression in the Qur'an appears both for warning and threatening, and for encouragement and creating eagerness. Here, the context shows that it is for encouragement and creating eagerness. The meaning is: "Have you heard the story of Moses? We narrate this story to you. Within it, you will find the answers to all those questions that confront you at this stage of the mission, or which will confront you in the subsequent phases." It should be clear that the reason why the story of Moses (peace be upon him) and his nation is repeated in various styles in the Qur'an is that the inheritance of Moses's Ummah was transferred to the Ummah of the Prophet (peace be upon him). Before the Prophet (peace be upon him), the law-giving prophet was Moses (peace be upon him). The heart-rending phases he faced in the formation of his Ummah, and the manner in which Allah helped him, were highly instructive for the Prophet (peace be upon him). The slips the Children of Israel made at every step, and the instructions Allah gave for their reform and training, serve as a lesson for this Ummah. Do not overlook this significance when reading this story; this is not merely a tale of others, but a narrative of our own self.

"When he saw a fire and said to his family, 'Stay here; indeed, I have perceived a fire. Perhaps I can bring you a burning brand from it or find guidance at the fire.'"

**Moses's Story from the Beginning of His Prophethood:**

This starts the story of Moses (peace be upon him) from the moment he was granted prophethood. On his return from Midian, when he arrived near the Valley of Tuwa with his wife and children, it was night. They could not determine the path, and it appears that the cold was severe. Suddenly, he saw a flame from one direction. Moses (peace be upon him) said to his family: "You stay here; I have seen a flame. I will go there, and either I will bring an ember from it for you to warm yourselves—as specified in Surah Al-Qasas—or if there are people there, I will find out the path from them, and we can continue our journey."

**Observing a Fleeting Flame:**

From the word "anastu" (I perceived), it is deduced that the nature of the fire he saw was not a burning campfire, but a fleeting flame that flashed and disappeared. This is why this word is used for catching a glimpse or perceiving. Just a flash was seen, and it suddenly disappeared. Perhaps no one saw it except Moses (peace be upon him). If it were a blazing fire, Moses (peace be upon him) would have said: "Look, there is a fire burning over there; I will go and find the path from there, and if no one is there, I will get some means for us to warm ourselves."

"And when he came to it, he was called, 'O Moses, Indeed, I am your Lord, so remove your sandals. Indeed, you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa.'"

When Moses arrived at the place where he had seen the flame, he was called: "O Moses, indeed I am your Lord." That is, you came here seeking fire, but here there is no fire; rather, here is your Lord.

**The Instruction of Humility and its Etiquette:**

"So remove your sandals" — the first instruction was to remove the shoes. Presenting oneself with removed shoes is not only an etiquette of cleanliness, but also of humility. Consequently, this is considered necessary for the respect of our mosques as well, because the mosque is the place of presenting oneself before God, for which both cleanliness and humility are essential. Some people assume that if the shoes are clean, there is no harm in entering the mosque with them, but this is not correct because it is contrary to humility. For presenting oneself in the mosque, just as cleanliness is a condition, humility is an even greater condition.

"Indeed, you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa" — this explains the reason for the instruction: "You are currently in the sacred valley of Tuwa." Tuwa is the name of the valley situated at the foot of Mount Sinai in the Sinai Peninsula. The sanctity of any mountain, valley, or piece of land is a relative matter. All land is created by God; no piece has inherent superiority over another, except when that piece of land is honored with a special connection to Allah. This valley achieved this special honor because here Allah Almighty blessed Moses with His manifestation and His speech, which is why it achieved the status of sanctity.

"And I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed [to you]."

"Istima'" means to listen with attention and care. This sentence is meant to draw complete attention before stating the actual message: "I have chosen you for My special task—the duty of prophethood and messengership—so listen carefully and attentively to what is revealed to you." Within this, there is also an indication of the honor and distinction Moses received due to being blessed with the status of prophethood, and also of the massive responsibility that fell upon him by virtue of this status.

"Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity except Me, so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance."

**The Primary Teaching to Moses:**

This is the primary teaching given to Moses, and this is the teaching that has always been given to all prophets. If we analyze this verse, we find that three things are mentioned in it:

First, the creed of monotheism (Tawheed), because this creed is the center of religion. As far as believing in God is concerned, the world has always believed in God; the foolishness of atheism is a product of modern times. However, the misguidance of shirk has always attacked human in one form or another, which is why every prophet was taught monotheism first, and every prophet waged jihad against shirk first.

Second, Moses was instructed to worship God. This worship is the right of Allah, and this right belongs to Him without any partner. Since He is the Deity, it is binding to worship Him; and since He has no partner, it is also binding that this worship be completely free of partners. Among the requirements of this worship, as we have mentioned elsewhere, is obedience to God. Just as it is impermissible to worship anyone other than God, it is impermissible to willingly obey anyone in opposition to God's commands. The true spirit of worship is acknowledging one's servitude and slavery before God, renewing one's covenant of obedience and loyalty with Him, and remembering Him. For this reason, it is entirely contrary to servitude to glorify Him but compile a law of one's own, or to accept this right for anyone else; this is shirk. The arguments for this being shirk have been described in our treatises *Haqeeqat-e-Shirk* and *Haqeeqat-e-Tawheed*.

Third, prayer. For prayer, the word "iqamah" (establishing) is used, within which lies the concept of meticulous care. The command for prayer is given to keep the remembrance of Allah alive and established. Whether prayer is individual or collective, it is both the greatest guardian of God's remembrance and its highest and most effective manifestation. For this reason, immediately after the teaching of monotheism, all prophets were instructed with this first. It is through prayer that a servant remembers the covenant of servitude his Lord took from him, and which he made with his Lord; and it is through this that any Ummah remembers the covenant God took from it, and which it acknowledged to God. If any individual or Ummah wastes prayer, they have wasted the entire religion. This subject was clarified under Surah Maryam, verse 59; please refer to that as well.

"Indeed, the Hour is coming - I almost conceal it - so that every soul may be recompensed for that which it strives."

**Reminding of the Resurrection:**

This is a reminder of the Resurrection, and for its arrival, the active participle "atiyah" is used, which means not just that it will come, but carries emphasis and certainty that it *is* coming; it is inevitable and fixed. It must be noted that the general mindset regarding the Resurrection has never been one of open denial, but of astonishment and finding it far-fetched. For this reason, alongside proving it, the Qur'an has placed great emphasis on its certainty.

**The Purpose of the Resurrection:**

"so that every soul may be recompensed for that which it strives" — this describes the primary purpose of the Resurrection: its arrival is inevitable because it is necessary that every soul be recompensed for its earning, whether good or bad. If this were not so, this world would not be the work of a Wise and Merciful Creator, but the play of a child, and this is self-evidently contrary to intellect. The phrase "bima tas'a" (for that which it strives) also clarifies the reality that it is necessary that every person receive the recompense of their *own* actions. If someone's actions are those of Satan, but through the strength of intercession or their lineage they achieve the status of the righteous and are spared from accountability, it would mean—refuge is with Allah—that before Allah, the actual value is not of actions and character, but of intercession and lineage.

**An Eloquent Parenthesis:**

Within the verse, the words "I almost conceal it" serve as a parenthetical statement. This parenthesis is highly eloquent. It does not merely say "I will conceal the Hour," but "I almost conceal it." Those with a taste for Arabic can appreciate that the word "akadu" (almost) introduces the meaning that: "Though I have currently placed a veil over the Resurrection, and will continue to do so, the Resurrection itself is entirely impatient to reveal itself." The same subject is expressed in Surah Al-A'raf as: "Heavy it is through the heavens and the earth. It will not come upon you except unexpectedly." (7:187)

"So let no one avert you from it who does not believe in it and follows his desire, for you would then perish."

**An Example of Concise Expression:**

In our view, the pronoun in "'anha" (from it) refers to the prayer (Salah), and the pronoun in "biha" refers to the Hour (the Resurrection). That is: "Let no one avert you or make you negligent of prayer who does not believe in the Resurrection and follows his desire." In this interpretation, although the question of split pronouns (intishar-e-dhameer) arises, which is normally considered a defect in speech, it is a defect only when there is no clear contextual clue to determine the reference of each pronoun. If a clear clue is present, it is not a defect; rather, it introduces the beauty of conciseness (Ijaz) into the text, and excellent examples of this exist in the speech of the eloquent.

Here, the clue is entirely clear. After the mention of prayer and the Resurrection, two verbs are used: "yasuddanna" (avert) and "yu'min" (believe). Anyone with a taste for the language can naturally understand that the first verb has a clear relationship with prayer, and the second with the Resurrection. This was the view of Abu Muslim among the predecessors, and it appears correct to me in every respect.

**The Relationship Between Prayer and Belief in the Hereafter:**

Reflecting on this matter from the perspective of the wisdom of religion, it is a reality that for a person who does not possess firm belief in the Hereafter, prayer is a burden. This is specified in the Qur'an in verses 45-46 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "And indeed, it is extremely difficult except for the humbly submissive [to Allah] - who are certain that they will meet their Lord and that they will return to Him."

Similarly, it is mentioned in the Qur'an that following desires and wasting prayer are inseparably linked. In Surah Maryam, verse 59 states: "But there came after them successors who neglected prayer and pursued desires; so they will soon meet evil."

**The Jews Wasted Both Prayer and Belief in the Hereafter:**

These verses make it clear that immediately after monotheism, the first thing Moses was emphasized with was prayer, and the specific thing reminded of was the Hereafter. This is consistent with the history of the prophets, as you have seen in Surah Maryam. But the wretchedness of the Jews is lamentable: they wasted prayer and forgot the Hereafter. You will be surprised to learn that in their scriptures, while sacrifices are mentioned, prayer—specifically in the Temple—is mentioned in only one place, in Deuteronomy 26. Some of their jurists even hold the view that Moses never commanded prayer at all; if it is mentioned in the Torah, it is an invention of later compilers. As for the Hereafter, their Sadducees do not believe in the resurrection after death at all. Their creed is that reward and punishment are fully meted out in this world alone. It appears that our Murji'ah borrowed their creed from them.

Finally, the word "fatarda" (for you would then perish) serves as a warning: "If you become negligent of prayer and the remembrance of the Hereafter, know that you will be ruined." These are the very practices that guard the entire religion and keep one safe from the trials of Satan. One who becomes negligent of them effectively hands himself over to Satan.

"And what is that in your right hand, O Moses?"

**A Question of Affection and Kindness:**

The word "yameen" (right hand), stripped of its right-versus-left meaning, is also used to denote the hand in general, of which examples exist in Arabic speech.

This question was obviously not asked to seek information, but to show affection and kindness. Just as a father asks his child: "My son, what is that in your hand?" though he knows what the object is better than the child. Similarly, the Generous Lord asked Moses: "Moses, what is that in your hand?" and asked regarding the very object that was to become the medium for all those wonders that manifested through Moses's hands.

"He said, 'It is my staff; I lean upon it, and I bring down leaves for my sheep with it, and I have therein other uses.'"

**The Meaning of "Ahushshu":** "Hass" means to shake leaves off a tree. Al-'Ukbari interpreted "ahushshu biha 'ala ghanami" as "aqoomu biha 'ala ghanami" — meaning: "With this staff, I shepherd and guard my flock." If lexicography supported this meaning, it would be excellent, but Al-'Ukbari provided no evidence. For this reason, I have followed the well-known meaning.

Moses replied: "This is my staff upon which I lean when necessary, with which I shake leaves off trees for my sheep, and I have other uses for it as well."

One can clearly feel that Moses did not content himself with merely giving a simple, direct answer to the question; rather, his answer carries a tone of conversational joy and expansion of speech. He could have simply replied: "It is my staff," but he perceived from the manner of the question that it was not merely an inquiry, but carried the delight of special affection. For this reason, he detailed his answer, hoping to open the way for further conversation. When the addresser is beloved and sought-after, the desire to prolong the conversation is a natural human instinct.

*The story was delightful, so I told it at greater length!*

"[Allah] said, 'Throw it down, O Moses.' So he threw it down, and thereupon it was a snake, moving swiftly."

**The Miracle of the Staff:**

The command came: "Throw this staff upon the ground, and observe the wonder of divine power!" Consequently, Moses threw the staff upon the ground, and it suddenly became a swiftly moving snake.

"[Allah] said, 'Seize it and fear not; We will return it to its former condition.'"

To fear upon seeing a snake is a natural human reaction, and consequently, Moses was afraid when he saw this sight—that the staff in his hand, which could be used to kill a snake, had itself become a snake. But Allah Almighty reassured him: "Do not fear; seize it without hesitation, and as soon as you touch it, We will return it to its original state." It would become the same staff it was before.

"And draw your hand to your side; it will come out white without disease - another sign."

**The Miracle of the White Hand:**

Along with this, the second instruction was: "Draw your hand under your armpit; then when you bring it out, it will emerge pure white, without any disease, as a second sign."

**Refutation of a Statement in the Torah:**

This was the second miracle granted to Moses. Here, the condition "min ghayri soo'" (without disease) is meant to dispel the doubt that this whiteness of the hand was due to some illness; rather, it was a sign from Allah. This refutes the narrative in the Torah claiming that when Moses brought his hand out, it was white with leprosy. It should also be remembered that this whiteness of Moses's hand was not permanent; rather, the words of the Qur'an bear witness that Allah specified its manifestation with the condition that when Moses put his hand under his armpit and brought it out as a sign, only then would it emerge white. The doubt of an illness would occur if the hand remained permanently white. But if that were so, where would the miracle be?

"Ayatan ukhra" (another sign) is not a separate sentence, but a second state (Hal). One state is that it will come out pure white without disease, and the second is that it will emerge as another sign. We have maintained this in the translation.

"That We may show you [some] of Our greatest signs."

This is glad tidings for Moses regarding future victories: "Though these appear to be only two miracles, they are not limited to this; rather, within them are enclosed many of Our other great miracles. In the future, when the stages of trial arrive, you will see what wonders and x-factors of Our power and majesty manifest through them." Consequently, the wonders that manifested through them against Pharaoh are detailed in the Torah. The grandeur of the wonders mentioned in the Qur'an itself is apparent: this very staff carved a path through the sea for them, and with it, he struck a rock to gush forth twelve springs.

**The Reason for the Special Treatment of Moses Regarding Miracles:**

Immediately after being taught and emphasized with monotheism, the Hereafter, and prayer, and being granted these two miracles, Moses was commanded to go to Pharaoh to discharge his mission of messengership and warn him of God and His wrath, as he had become extremely rebellious. Here, a question arises: the established divine way regarding miracles has been that they were granted to prophets when their nations intensely demanded them, and the purpose was to establish final proof (itmaam al-hujjah), leaving no excuse for those who were not using their intellect and insisted on a miracle. Why then was this special treatment meted out to Moses, where he was granted two miracles immediately upon being commissioned with prophethood?

In our view, the reason is that Moses was being sent as a messenger to a rebellious and tyrannical ruler who was a mortal enemy of Moses, both personally and nationally. Far from listening to him or understanding him, there was apprehension that as soon as he learned that this was Moses, he would immediately order his execution. In fact, he had already ordered his execution when the event of killing the Egyptian occurred, but Moses escaped to Midian, which is why he failed in his plan. If Moses had gone to such an avenging tyrant merely as a messenger to warn him, why would he be willing to listen to him! He could only be willing to listen if something manifested through Moses's hands that would awe him. Consequently, Allah armed him in the very beginning with two such miracles through which he remained safe from every transgression of his enemy, and as soon as he went before Pharaoh—as will be clear from the subsequent verses—he displayed his miracles so that Pharaoh would be warned that if he took any wrong step, Moses had not come empty-handed, but held in his hand that staff which was fully sufficient to smash every pride and arrogance.

"Go to Pharaoh. Indeed, he has transgressed."

**Pharaoh's Rebellion:**

This is the first mission Moses was commissioned with. The command came: "Go to Pharaoh; indeed, he has become extremely rebellious." The details of Pharaoh's rebellion have passed in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf. In his presumption, he considered himself the supreme lord, and was regarded as the manifestation of the Egyptians' supreme deity, the sun. His statues and idols were worshipped throughout Egypt. To estimate his treatment of God's servants, it is sufficient to note that he had issued a decree throughout his kingdom that every son born to the Children of Israel be killed or thrown into the river, with only the girls kept alive to serve as maids. For someone whose attitude towards the Creator and creation was this, what room remains to dispute his rebellion! Since Moses was well aware of this rebellion—indeed, he himself was a victim of it—Allah did not detail it here, but confined Himself to a brief indication.

**A Fundamental Difference Between the Call of Prophets and Public Movements:**

Keep also in mind here the point to which we have indicated in various places: that the prophets make the chiefs, elders, and leaders of their nation and society the primary target of their call and warning, because the system of society runs under their leadership. If they accept the reform, the entire society accepts it very quickly, and the awe of the call's rationality and superiority dominates everyone. And if they reject the truth due to pride and obstinacy, this too is beneficial for the call from one perspective, because the hollowness of their leaders' logic becomes clear to their followers, and when they subsequently accept the truth, they do so with full consciousness and complete insight. This insight develops within them such a power of conquest that if yesterday they were like fishermen catching fish, after this revolution of state they become fishers of men. In contrast, movements that start their work through public propaganda depend entirely on emotional slogans that incite the public. They run on these slogans, and as long as they stand, they stand on them. They contain no share of intellect, nor can they. While the role of emotions as an effective factor cannot be denied, if the reins of life are handed over to them, the end of life is well-known!

"[Moses] said, 'My Lord, expand for me my breast. And ease for me my task. And untie the knot from my tongue. That they may understand my speech. And appoint for me a minister from my family - Aaron, my brother. Increase through him my strength. And let him share my task. That we may glorify You much. And remember You much. Indeed, You are of us ever Seeing.'"

**The Difference Between a Prophet and Arrogant Worldly Leaders:**

As soon as Moses realized that the responsibilities of messengership had been placed upon him, and that he had to go to Pharaoh as a messenger of God to warn and propagate, he immediately made this prayer quoted above. From every word of this prayer, one can estimate how heavily he felt this burden, and with what deep concern he pleaded to Allah Almighty for help and guidance in discharging these great responsibilities. The state of worldly leaders is that they live for the lust of leadership and die in its love, but the breasts of the prophets are entirely free of this lust. When Allah Almighty commissions them with the status of leadership, they tremble at the thought of its responsibilities, and pray to God that since He has placed this burden upon them, He Himself should grant them the courage and strength to bear it, and guide and support them at every step.

**The Guidance of expanding the Breast:**

"My Lord, expand for me my breast" — first, Moses prayed for the expansion of his breast (Sharh-e-Sadr). If a responsibility is heavy and a person deeply feels this responsibility, every sensitive person's breast is tightened by it; this is no proof of weakness, but of being sensitive and duty-conscious. The prophets do not receive prophethood by demanding it; rather, its responsibility, as we mentioned, is placed upon them by Allah, which is why they naturally experience anxiety and hesitation in their breasts as to whether they will be able to discharge this responsibility fully. Obviously, this tightness of the breast can be removed only by Allah's grace. He is the One Who places courage and determination in the heart, and He is the One Who grants the light of knowledge and certainty for the subsequent phases, upon which the breast is expanded and courage is raised. This reality is maintained here: that even upon the prophets, all knowledge and certainty are not poured in a single day; rather, they too receive their provisions according to the stages of their journey, and continuously recite: "My Lord, increase me in knowledge."

**Prayer for the Success of the Mission:**

"And ease for me my task" — after the prayer for removing the inner tightness, this is a prayer for the favorableness of circumstances, the smoothing of the path, and the success of the mission: "Though this mission is exceptionally difficult, if You will, You can make every difficulty easy; so ease this task for me by Your grace, and remove every obstacle from the path."

**Request for the Gift of Expression and Speech:**

"And untie the knot from my tongue, that they may understand my speech" — in a highly polite and humble manner, this is a request for being granted the power of expression and speech for the purpose of the mission. During that era, eloquence and speech were highly valued. In Arabic society, the orator of a tribe was its chief and leader. Without the gift of speech during that era, no person could fully discharge the responsibilities of leadership and command. Today, the invention of the press has greatly increased the importance of the pen; indeed, today the primary importance belongs to the press. Today, if a person manages to secure the aid of the press, they can claim to be a greater orator than Demosthenes, even if they cannot speak two sentences correctly. But before the invention of the press, eloquence and speech held massive importance. Specifically, those with the responsibility of leading and commanding the public could not discharge their responsibility without this capability. The Qur'an informs us that Moses was not an eloquent orator. Although eloquence is not among the requirements of prophethood, its importance for the purpose of propagation, as we mentioned, was massive during that era, which is why when the responsibility of messengership was placed upon him, he felt this deficiency within himself and prayed, alongside the expansion of his breast, for being granted the power of expression: "O Lord, grant my tongue fluency and strength so that I may present my call in such a manner that people listen to my words and understand them."

Due to the feeling of this deficiency, he also prayed for his elder brother, Aaron, to be made his partner. Both the Torah and the Qur'an inform us that Aaron was an exceptionally powerful orator. Moses naturally expected that if his support was secured, the deficiency he felt within himself would be compensated. In Surah Al-Qasas, his prayer is mentioned as: "And my brother Aaron is more fluent than me in tongue, so send him with me as a helper to support me. Indeed, I fear that they will deny me." (28:34)

This verse makes it absolutely clear that the actual need Moses felt for the purpose of the mission was for a powerful orator. From this perspective, he felt a deficiency within himself and wished to be supported by an orator more fluent than himself, so that he could present his call before the people in a better and more effective manner. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 13 states: "And my breast is tight and my tongue is not fluent, so send for Aaron."

**Did Moses Have a Lisp?**

These verses certainly inform us that Moses was not a fluent and powerful orator, but it is nowhere stated that he had a lisp. The narrative of a lisp exists only in the Talmud and biblical folklore. From there, it entered our commentary books, and then to validate it, our commentators constructed a strange and absurd story of Moses eating a hot coal as an infant. The state of these biblical narratives is apparent to you: Moses's white hand was turned into a symptom of leprosy. For people who could turn their prophet into a leper, what is so surprising if they also find a lisping defect in him!

At any rate, there is nothing in the Qur'an to support the narrative of a lisp. In "wa-hlul 'uqdatan min lisanee yafqahoo qawlee", the request is not to cure a lisp, but to be granted that capability of expression which was necessary for discharging the duties of prophethood, and this request is in a highly humble style. Moses did not say: "Make me a spellbinding orator to conquer hearts," but in a highly humble manner: "Grant my tongue that fluency that people may understand my words." There is no need to be a lisping patient to make this prayer; rather, even an eloquent master of language makes this prayer, and should do so. Frequently, when the flow of meaning and truth surges in the breast, even an eloquent master feels his tongue and pen to be deficient in expressing it. Moses did not merely have to raise slogans like common leaders; he had to explain the deep truths and secrets of religion, and that too before people who were not only bent on denying him but were his mortal enemies. Furthermore, Moses did not merely wish to discharge his duty somehow, but desired to enter the hearts of his opponents. Since he could not expect his opponents to open their hearts, he asked his Lord for a powerful tongue so he could make them understand his words.

Therefore, in our view, the narrative of a lisp is unreliable. Apart from the fact that Allah Almighty preserves His prophets from physical defects just as He preserves them from moral defects, the most important thing is that there is nothing in the Qur'an to support this narrative. As for Pharaoh's taunt quoted in Surah Az-Zukhruf as "la yakadu yubeen" (he can barely speak clearly), we will discuss that in its place, if Allah wills.

**The Reason for the Prayer to Make Aaron a Partner:**

From the verses of Surah Al-Qasas and Ash-Shu'ara referenced above, it is clear that Moses requested a helper and partner specifically from the perspective of his own deficiency in expression, and for the compensation of this deficiency, he regarded his brother Aaron as the most suitable person. He was his elder brother, Moses trusted his character, and above all, Aaron was famous in the nation for his eloquence. When a person characterized by all these qualities was present within his own house and family, he was the most worthy of Moses praying for him to be made his partner. From the words "min ahlee" (from my family), let no one fall into the misunderstanding that this choice was influenced by family nepotism. The doubt of nepotism would occur if lineage alone were the reason for the choice; but when the reason is capability and qualities, not only is there no room for this doubt, but it teaches us that when a person prays to Allah for some favor and mercy, he should first include his family members, provided they are capable and righteous. Please refer to Abraham's prayer in the commentary of Al-Baqarah.

**The Status of Aaron:**

"ashdud bihi azree wa ashrik-hu fee amree" — "azr" means the back and waist; meaning: "Make Aaron my minister and partner to strengthen my back, so that I may bear this heavy burden with honor and goodness." From "wa ashrik-hu fee amree" (and let him share my task), it also follows that Moses's request was for Aaron's help not merely as a companion, but as a partner in prophethood. That is, he too should be answerable before Allah regarding the duties of prophethood, and he too should receive direct guidance and protection from Allah. This request was a highly significant request, because among the prophets, as we mentioned under Surah Maryam, verse 53, it is not known for any other prophet that another prophet was made his minister. But Allah Almighty honored Moses with this and accepted his request. Among worldly leaders, there exists such jealousy and rivalry even for minor posts that they do not tolerate anyone else sharing their power; but where there is a true sense of trust and responsibility, a prophet prays to Allah to share even his great office of prophethood with another.

Note here that in the Torah, Aaron is not mentioned with the status he has in the Qur'an. In the Torah, he is mentioned merely as a companion and spokesman of Moses, and that too as a companion upon whom the responsibility for the golden calf mischief is placed. It is the Qur'an's favor that it has highlighted the true status of these personalities.

**The Meaning of "Dhikr":**

"key nusabbihaka katheeran wa nadhkuraka katheeran" — meaning, the request to share the office of prophethood with Aaron is so that we may both glorify You much and remember You much. Obviously, individual glorification and remembrance is not the only thing meant here; collective glorification and remembrance is also meant, and this is an expression of discharging the duties of prophethood. The privacy and publicity of a prophet and those who work on the path of prophets is entirely glorification and remembrance. The work they do in the mosque is glorification and remembrance, and the work they do in the market is also glorification and remembrance. The difference in their fields of action is not of the nature of the action, but of the field of action. In verse 14 of this Surah, you read: "and establish prayer for My remembrance," and then in verse 42, both Moses and Aaron are commanded to go to Pharaoh's court, along with the instruction: "and do not slacken in My remembrance." Obviously, this remembrance refers to the warning and propagation for which they were being sent to Pharaoh.

**A Delicate Difference Between "Tasbeeh" (Glorification) and "Dhikr" (Remembrance):**

In mentioning both "Tasbeeh" and "Dhikr" together, a delicate literary aspect is also maintained, which should be kept in mind. Within "Tasbeeh", the aspect of negation (Tanzih) is dominant, and within "Dhikr", the aspect of affirmation (Ithbat); and both these aspects are necessary to establish a correct connection with God. Those things that are contrary to God's majesty, His attributes, and His pleasure should be negated, and those things that are in accordance with His majesty, His attributes, and His commands should be affirmed and expressed. From both these things, the creed and character of a believer are formed, and negation precedes affirmation. Until you declare rebellion against everything other than Allah, you cannot be loyal to Allah. Before the declaration "illa Allah" (except Allah), the declaration "la ilaha" (there is no deity) is necessary.

**A Recommendation on Behalf of the Prayer:**

"Indeed, You are of us ever Seeing" — this is a recommendation on behalf of this prayer, similar to the recommendation Zachariah presented on behalf of his prayer with the words: "and I have never been disappointed when calling upon You, my Lord." We explained this in the commentary of Surah Maryam. In the court of God, the best recommendation for a human is not his own action, but the mediation of God's own grace and mercy. When a servant asks his Lord with the trust that "He Who granted everything without asking will surely not deprive me upon asking," the Generous Lord does not leave His servant deprived. Moses also referenced his Lord's treatment of him and his brother: "Since You have always been our Guardian, now that we are rising for Your name's sake, how shall we remain deprived of Your grace!"

**The Request is Accepted:**

"[Allah] said, 'You have been granted your request, O Moses.'" — "su'l" and "su'al" carry the same meaning. That is, the request and plea. The command came: "Moses, your request is accepted." Within this address "O Moses", there is great affection, and the words of acceptance also display great majesty. As soon as the prayer's words ended, the glad tiding of acceptance was delivered: "Your petition is accepted!" As if with a single stroke of the pen, without any delay or exception, everything was accepted! Obviously, within this acceptance was included the request for being granted the power of expression; that too was granted, and Aaron was made his minister. If a prayer is made for the right purpose, at the right time, and with sincere emotion, it receives honor of acceptance from God's court in this very manner!

"And We had already conferred favor upon you another time. When We inspired to your mother what was inspired, [Saying], 'Cast him into the chest and cast it into the river, and the river will cast it onto the bank; there will take him an enemy to Me and an enemy to him.' And I bestowed upon you love from Me that you would be brought up under My eye."

**Reminding of a Past Special Favor:**

To remove Moses's anxiety, just as the glad tiding of his prayer's acceptance was delivered, he was reminded of that special favor Allah Almighty conferred upon him at the time of his birth, when there was a danger of him falling victim to the tyrannical scheme of killing Israeli children being run by Pharaoh. Allah Almighty, through His power and wisdom, not only saved him, but had him brought up by Pharaoh himself. The purpose of reminding him of this event is that he should have peace of mind: "Why should any fear enter your heart regarding the Pharaoh against whom such wonders of God's protection have already been displayed for you? Your Lord is with you."

**Divine Arrangements for Moses's Protection:**

"When We inspired to your mother what was inspired" — this details the special favor: "For your protection, the plan We approved was inspired to your mother, and that inspiration is what is being revealed to you now, so that it may be clear to you how great Our past favors upon you have been." Although the basic knowledge of this event must have been known to Moses through his mother, the underlying operations of the Hand of the Unseen were fully known only through this revelation. The word "ma yooha" (what was inspired) points to this aspect.

**What was Inspired to Moses's Mother?**

The inspiration that came to Moses's mother was obviously of the nature of intuition and inspiration (Ilham). That is, Allah placed a matter into her heart, and she also achieved the peace of mind that this was placed by God. "Cast him into the chest and cast it into the river" — this is the inspiration that was given to Moses's mother. The Book of Exodus (2:2-6) informs us that during the era of Moses's birth, the scheme of killing Israeli children was running with full force. Moses's mother hid the child from the oppressors for some time (three months, according to the Torah), but she was constantly afraid that if the Pharaoh's people found out, the child would not be safe. The Torah's narrative is that she ultimately made a basket of bulrushes, placed the child in it, and laid the basket in the flags by the river's brink, hoping that some compassionate person might see the child and save his life. The Qur'an's statement is that Allah Almighty placed it into the heart of Moses's mother to place the child in a chest and cast it into the river, and not to fear or grieve: "We will return the child to you and make him Our messenger." Obviously, the Qur'an's statement is more rational. Without a divine indication, how could Moses's mother find the heart to take such a massive step? Even after the divine indication, what her motherly instinct must have suffered is beyond words; but since the divine indication had also assured her: "Do this; Allah Himself will arrange for the child's protection," she placed a stone on her heart and cast the chest into the waves of the river. In Surah Al-Qasas, verse 7 clarifies this divine indication: "And when you fear for him, cast him into the river and do not fear and do not grieve. Indeed, We will return him to you and will make him [one] of the messengers."

"and the river will cast it onto the bank; there will take him an enemy to Me and an enemy to him" — this is not part of the inspiration given to Moses's mother. If this were also part of the inspiration, its form would have been that of a responsive command, whereas "fal-yulqihi" has a lam of imperative, which is a third-person command. The literal translation is: "and let the river cast it onto the bank." That is, to the mother, Allah inspired to place the child in a chest and cast it into the river, and He commanded the river to cast it onto the bank. "He will be picked up by one who is both My enemy and his enemy." This is Allah displaying for Moses His power, wisdom, and plan: "We arranged for your protection and upbringing in such a manner that We had you cast into the river by your mother, and then willed that the river cast you onto the bank, and thus, your and My enemy—Pharaoh—brought you up." 

*An enemy becomes the medium of good, if God wills!*

**A Wonder of Power:**

Within "an enemy to Me and an enemy to him" lies proof of God's perfect control: that Allah Almighty had Moses protected and brought up by his and His own enemy. He was His enemy because he claimed divinity; how could he tolerate any God other than himself? And his enmity with Moses has been detailed. Furthermore, it is a wonder of His wisdom and plan that He had His and His nation's greatest enemy brought up by Pharaoh himself, and Pharaoh, with his own hands, arranged for the very danger to escape which he had exhausted all efforts!

**The Reflection of Divine Love Upon Moses:**

"And I bestowed upon you love from Me" — this is the divine plan Allah adopted to make Moses beloved in the eyes of his and His enemy. It is stated: "We cast upon you a reflection of Our love." A child is naturally attractive, and every person of sound nature feels compassion upon seeing a child; and Moses, as established by Torah narratives, was also exceptionally beautiful. Then, the additional divine favor was that Allah cast His love upon him. Who can estimate the attractiveness of a child upon whom lies the reflection of divine love! When such an attractive child is lying in a basket of bulrushes, cast up by the river, alone, the very picture of innocence and beauty, whose heart would not be moved to compassion! Pharaoh, after all, was human, not a stone! Consequently, through this divine plan, Moses, who had been cast into the Nile, reached the palace of Pharaoh for his upbringing—the very Pharaoh for fear of whom his mother had separated him from her chest and cast him into the chest of the river!

"that you would be brought up under My eye" — here, the conjunction based on contextual clues indicates that the conjunct is omitted. If we expand it, the complete statement would be: "We cast upon you the reflection of Our love so that your enemies should love you, and that your upbringing should occur under Our special supervision and before Our eyes." Obviously, it was this reflection of Allah's love that served as Moses's guardian, and this guardian protected him in such a manner that not only was there no danger to him from his mortal enemy, but the enemy became devoted to him. This guardian love is what Allah has described here as "under My eye". By casting His love upon him, Allah effectively placed His watch over Moses.

"[And We favored you] when your sister went and said, 'Shall I direct you to one who will be responsible for him?' So We returned you to your mother that her eye might be cooled and she would not grieve. And you killed a man, but We saved you from retaliation and tried you with many trials. And you remained [some] years among the people of Midian. Then you came [here] at the decreed time, O Moses."

In this verse, a massive portion of Moses's story is summarized. Those details that were not necessary for reminding on this occasion are omitted, and only a brief reference is made to some matters, after which the discourse connects back to the main subject.

**The Divine Plan for Returning Moses to His Mother:**

"when your sister went..." — this refers to the plan Allah adopted to return Moses to his mother after he was taken from the river. We mentioned under Al-Qasas: 7 that when Allah instructed Moses's mother to cast the child into the river, He also assured her: "We will return the child to you." This is a brief reference to the manner in which this promise was fulfilled. The details in Surah Al-Qasas (9-13) are that when Pharaoh's wife saw the child, she said to Pharaoh: "A very attractive child. Do not let him be killed; he is a cooling of the eyes for me and you. He will be of use to us, or we can take him as a son." Meanwhile, the state of Moses's mother was that though she had cast the child into the river by divine inspiration, her heart was breaking with grief. She instructed Moses's sister to keep track of where the chest drifted, watching from a distance. Ultimately, she learned that the chest reached Pharaoh's palace, and the river cast it onto the bank, and Pharaoh and his wife picked up the child. Moses's sister went to Pharaoh's palace and saw that they were trying to feed the child from various wet-nurses, but the child was restless, refusing to take any breast. Moses's sister presented a proposal to Pharaoh's wife: "If you agree, I can direct you to a household who will take excellent care of this child and make him comfortable." Since Pharaoh and his household were deeply anxious due to Moses refusing to nurse, this proposal was accepted, and thus, Moses returned to his mother's chest.

In the Torah, this event is described as:

"Then said his sister to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee? And Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother. And Pharaoh's daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it." (Exodus 2:7-9)

There is no need to comment on the difference between the Torah and the Qur'an's statement. Any intelligent person can estimate that the Qur'an's statement is rational and natural. It is regrettable that the compilers of the Torah were unfamiliar with the true events of their own household.

Here, in "idh tamshee ukhtuka fataqoolu", present-tense verbs are used. This suggests to me that Moses's sister had to visit Pharaoh's wife repeatedly to reassure her before she succeeded in convincing her of her proposal. Thus, Moses returned to his mother, and her eye was cooled. I have tried to maintain this in the translation.

**The Event of Killing the Egyptian:**

"And you killed a man, but We saved you from retaliation and tried you with many trials..." — of the events from his rescue from the river to his youth, only a reference is made here to the killing of the Egyptian and his journey to Midian. This reference is also for the same purpose as the previous reminder: to reassure Moses that the Lord Who helped and guided him in such dangerous and unfavorable circumstances will also help and guide him in the subsequent phases. Since only a brief reference is made to the events here, we will detail them in the light of Surah Al-Qasas, using our own words. The explanation of the verses and the research on their difficulties will appear in their proper place.

Placing verses 14-29 of Surah Al-Qasas before us, the events appear as follows:

When Moses reached youth and was granted knowledge and wisdom, he felt the humiliation and oppression of his nation deeply. The status of the Children of Israel in Egypt was that of slaves, which is why their settlement was separate from the main city. The Israelites went to the city only for labor and service, where they were severely beaten and humiliated at the hands of their Egyptian masters. Moses's zeal would flare up at these sights, and sometimes he would stand as a shield to defend his oppressed kinsmen. The Egyptians feared that if the Israelites found a leader to encourage them, they would become bold, and their mastery would be in danger. For this reason, they became enemies of Moses, and the government restricted his entry into the city. But Moses went to the city, avoiding the eyes of the enemies, to keep track of his kinsmen. One day, avoiding detection, he entered the city and saw an Israelite and an Egyptian fighting. The Israelite called out to Moses for help. Moses's zeal was aroused. He went to help the Israelite, and the Egyptian fought him. Moses was strong. The single blow of his fist he landed struck an unlucky spot, and the Egyptian fell dead on the spot.

Moses was deeply grieved by this accident, that an action had occurred from his hands without intention, for which he would be answerable before Allah. Consequently, he repented much, and Allah Almighty forgave his error. Upon this favor, he vowed: "I will never be a helper to criminals."

On the next day, avoiding detection, he went to the city again and saw the same Israelite who had sought help yesterday fighting another Egyptian. He called to Moses for help, but Moses rebuked him: "You are a troublesome man!" Nonetheless, he stepped forward to separate them. The Israelite assumed that Moses was stepping forward to seize him because he regarded him as troublesome. He was afraid that today Moses's fist might land on him. He cried out: "O Moses, yesterday you killed a man; do you intend to kill me today as well! If you have adopted this way, then this is not the way of a reformer; it appears you intend to become a tyrant in the land!"

From this, Moses realized that the event of killing the Egyptian was no longer a secret. Furthermore, a man came running from the far end of the city and informed Moses: "The chiefs of the government are planning your execution, so my sincere advice to you is to escape from here."

Moses prayed to be saved from the evil of the oppressors and, trusting in God's guidance, set out for Midian. Stopping near a well in Midian, he saw some shepherds watering their flocks, while two women were keeping their sheep back. Moses's chivalry was aroused, and he asked them: "Why are you keeping your flock back?" They replied: "Our father is an old man, and the task of shepherding falls upon us. We cannot compete in this rush, so we must wait until the shepherds finish before we can water our flock." Hearing this, Moses felt compassion. He stepped forward, watered their flock for them, and then sat beneath the shade of a tree, praying: "O my Lord, I am in need of whatever good You send down upon me!"

These two women were the daughters of Prophet Shuaib. After some time, one of the daughters came to Moses walking bashfully, and said: "My father is calling you so he may reward you for watering our flock." Moses presented himself to Shuaib and narrated his entire story. Shuaib reassured him: "Do not fear now; Allah has saved you from the oppressors."

Shuaib's daughter recommended to her father: "Employ him; he appears to be a strong and trustworthy man, and will prove to be an excellent servant."

Shuaib said to Moses: "I wish to marry one of these two daughters of mine to you on the condition that you serve me for eight years, and if you complete ten, it is of your own will. I will not impose any hardship upon you, and you will find me, if Allah wills, a good man."

Moses accepted this and said: "This agreement is settled between me and you. It is of my own will whether I serve eight years or ten; no hardship will be imposed upon me. We take Allah as witness over this agreement."

Completing the term of this agreement, when Moses was returning to Egypt, he saw that manifestation in the sacred valley of Tuwa, from which he went to get fire and returned with prophethood.

All these details of Surah Al-Qasas are presented here because this entire detail is summarized in the verse of Surah Ta-Ha under discussion. Until this detail is before us, the complete context of the verse does not emerge. Now reflect upon the parts of the verse:

**The Nature of Moses's Grief and His Slip:**

"And you killed a man, but We saved you from retaliation and tried you with many trials..." — the circumstances of killing the Egyptian are clear from the explanations above. Moses (peace be upon him) had no intention to kill, and had no motive other than defending an oppressed person. This motive is a highly praised and high motive, which exists in every person characterized by chivalry, honor, and zeal. For this reason, what Moses did was in itself correct. But by divine decree, his blow landed on such an unlucky spot that the Egyptian could not bear it and died. Moses realized that though it was without intention, an action had occurred from him for which he could be held answerable before Allah. This realization plunged him into deep grief. Those who fear God do not grant themselves easy excuses; rather, they hold themselves accountable even for those things for which they could easily find an excuse. Moses was also free of any blind national bias, which is why he must have also grieved that perhaps the Israelite he helped was the one in the wrong. Consequently, in the details above, you saw that when he saw the same Israelite fighting another Egyptian the next day, he rebuked him: "You are a troublesome man." If Moses had national bias, far from grieving, he would have been proud of performing a great feat in defense of his nation. But this was not the case, which is why he grieved and sought forgiveness from his Lord. Allah forgave him, and this forgiveness saved him from grief.

In our view, if the fear of the government's retaliation had any share in this grief, it was only from the perspective that the government was a highly tyrannical government from which no justice could be expected for an Israelite. If it were a just government, Moses would have presented the entire matter before it and abided by its decision; but handing oneself over to a tyrant like Pharaoh was synonymous with suicide. Obviously, this situation must also have been a cause of grief for Moses. To escape this grief, he ultimately left his homeland and went to Midian.

As far as the occurrence of this slip is concerned, we have explained in various places that if a slip occurs from the prophets before or after their mission, it is not of the nature of following desires; rather, in their zeal for truth or seeking truth, they sometimes exceed the required limit. This in itself is not an evil, but since the prophets are the touchstone of truth, Allah takes note of such things to keep the balance entirely correct. This slip of Moses was in reality a good deed, but when even a good deed exceeds its limits, it sometimes becomes worthy of note, specifically in the case of the prophets.

**The Trials of Moses:**

"And We tried you with many trials..." — "fatn" and "futoon" mean to test, try, and refine. The process of a goldsmith placing gold into a crucible to separate the dross from it is also called by this word. This is the word Allah has used for those trials in which He placed Moses to refine him, so that he might become fully worthy of the special task He willed to commission him with. His life in Egypt, and the dangerous circumstances under which he escaped, are clear; going to Midian, he spent eight or ten years shepherding Shuaib's sheep in the mountains and forests; departing from there, while still on the path, the great burden of messengership was placed upon him. After this, the heart-rending phases he faced have passed in Al-A'raf, and you will read them in this Surah and in Al-Qasas and other Surahs. In short, from birth to the final moment of life, his life was one of trials—each greater than the last!

**Trials are Necessary for Training:**

Regarding these trials, keep always in mind the reality that they are necessary for human training and education. Allah Almighty refines human's highest qualities through them, which is why they are not something to flee or fear. One who fears and flees them effectively fears and flees his own training. The thing to fear is not that a trial has presented itself, but that one might fail in that trial.

**The Divine Way Regarding Trials:**

These trials do not present themselves only through difficulties and misfortunes; they also present themselves in the form of blessings and favors. Through the trials of difficulties and misfortunes, Allah wills to train the servant in patience (Sabr), and through the trials of blessings and favors, He wills to refine his gratitude (Shukr). Upon these two qualities of patience and gratitude rests all high human character. Through the perfection of both these qualities, the rule of "the reassured soul" (nafs-e-mutma'innah) is established in the world, the fruit of which is that eternal kingdom described by the Qur'an as "well-pleased and pleasing to Him."

**A Deep Question of Qur'an Wisdom:**

Among the deep questions of Qur'anic wisdom is: between the trial of patience and the trial of gratitude, which is more difficult? The views of thinkers on this have varied. My own preference, in the light of the Qur'an, is that the trial of gratitude is more difficult. The average of those who pass it is very low. Many are found who remain firm on the truth in misfortunes, but very few are found who remain safe from going astray upon receiving blessings. The phrase "And few of My servants are grateful" (Saba: 13) points to this reality.

This reality should also be kept in mind: those whom Allah wills to elevate to the highest status are tested with the greatest and most severe trials. Read the details of the trials the prophets passed through, and the heart trembles. You and I cannot even conceive of bearing them.

*Those whose status is high face high difficulties!*

**The Thing to Actually Fear:**

Regarding these trials, the thing to actually fear is not that a trial presents itself or will present itself; rather, first, as we indicated, that a person should fear failing in any trial; and second, even more than this, that he should fear being excluded from the trial altogether. This is because the divine way is also that if a person continuously fails, Allah sometimes deprives him of His trial. In the terminology of the Qur'an, this is called "imhal" (granting respite/leeway), and this is the most severe punishment given to any individual or nation in this world. After this, all doors of hope are closed upon them.

**The Duration of the Stay in Midian:**

"And you remained [some] years among the people of Midian..." — there is no explicit clarification here as to how many years Moses remained in Midian, but in the light of Surah Al-Qasas, it is clear that under the agreement he made with Shuaib, he spent at least eight or ten years there, and during this time, he shepherded his sheep.

**The Phase of Prophethood:**

"Then you came [here] at the decreed time, O Moses" — after referencing all the past circumstances, it is stated: "All of this occurred in accordance with Our plan and Our pre-decreed program. Passing through all these phases, you arrived here precisely at the time We had pre-decreed for your presence, and We honored you with Our prophethood and messengership." The meaning is: "The God Who protected you in every phase from your birth until now, and fulfilled His plans for you through your and His enemies, is with you at every step; and the responsibility He is placing upon you, He Himself will open the paths for its discharge and He Himself will provide the means."

"And I have produced you for Myself."

The original meaning of "istina'" is to train and equip someone for a specific purpose. The meaning is: "I have tested you in so many crucibles and placed you through so many trials to train you for a special task of My own, and now I am commissioning you with that task; so bear this responsibility and step forward for its discharge with trust in Me."

"Go, you and your brother, with My signs and do not slacken in My remembrance."

This is a repetition of the command given in verse 24: "Go to Pharaoh and warn him." The intermediate verses were for Moses's reassurance. After delivering the glad tiding of his prayer's acceptance and referencing past favors, the instruction is repeated: "Go before Pharaoh with My signs, without any fear or anxiety." Previously, the command was given only to Moses, but now it is given to both Moses and Aaron, because upon Moses's request, Aaron had also been made a partner in prophethood.

**The Meaning of "Dhikr":**

"and do not slacken in My remembrance" — this is an emphasis of the instruction given in verse 14. The meaning is: "Do not become slack in the remembrance of God." As we mentioned under verse 34, "Dhikr" refers to both the remembrance of worship and the remembrance of propagation. Every act of a prophet is remembrance of God. When he prays, he secures through it the strength and knowledge for the reminding he does to creation. The true source of the call to truth is Allah Almighty. From this source, the call receives its nourishment and strength. If the connection with this source becomes weak, the call becomes lifeless and spiritless; and if the connection with this source is cut off, the call becomes entirely satanic, even if the name of God is taken in it.

"Go, both of you, to Pharaoh. Indeed, he has transgressed."

Pharaoh's rebellion was detailed under verse 24. Here, it is worthy of attention that the address is to both Moses and Aaron, which is why this is necessarily a subsequent phase when Moses had reached Egypt, because Aaron was not with Moses in the Valley of Tuwa.

"And speak to him with gentle speech that perhaps he may be reminded or fear [Allah]."

**The Method of Prophets' Call:**

This is an instruction regarding the method of call: "The call must be delivered with gentleness." The need for this instruction was not merely from the perspective that Moses was now going before Pharaoh not as a helpless Israeli, but as an ambassador of God, holding the staff of Moses; rather, gentleness (Layn) is the very nature of the call. The mission of the prophets is for teaching and reform, which is why a teacher's compassion and a sympathizer's concern are always apparent in their call and warning. It is not known of any prophet that he showed arrogance or tried to intimidate. In the most difficult circumstances, their style of address and manner of response remained exceptionally soft, effective, and sympathetic. Arrogance and intimidation are characteristics of worldly leaders. The entire foundation of modern satanic propaganda is built upon this.

**The True Objective of the Prophets' Call:**

"that perhaps he may be reminded or fear [Allah]" — the true objective of the prophets' call is that "tadhakkur" (remembrance) and "khashyah" (fear) be produced in the addressee. Those who are merely in a state of heedlessness wake up immediately through the prophet's reminding. They are like those who have lost their way; as soon as the guide directs them, they choose the straight path. There are others whose misguidance has become somewhat established. In such people, if they retain some capacity to accept the truth, the prophet's warning produces some fear of God and His grasp, and they begin to ponder the matter seriously, and if Allah's grace is with them, they choose the path of guidance. "Yatadhakkar" points to the first state, and "yakhsha" to the second.

"They said, 'Our Lord, indeed we fear that he will hasten [punishment] against us or that he will transgress.'"

**Expressing an Apprehension:**

Both of them pleaded: "O our Lord, we fear that before we can explain the matter, he will lay his hands upon us, or that upon hearing our words, he will become enraged and increase the oppression he has been committing against the Children of Israel." When "farat" is followed by "'ala", it means a hasty action against someone. The meaning is: "Pharaoh is already filled with rage. Why would he allow us to open our mouths! Before listening to us, he will either plan to eliminate us, or his rebellion will increase." Here, both Moses and Aaron are united in this plea. This supports the point we mentioned under verse 43: that these instructions were given after reaching Egypt, when Aaron had joined Moses.

"[Allah] said, 'Fear not. Indeed, I am with you both; I hear and I see.'"

**Reassurance from Allah Almighty:**

Allah Almighty reassured them: "Do not fear. Indeed, I am with you; I see and I hear." The detail enclosed within the brief phrase "I am with you" is beyond description; and within the words "I hear and I see", the majesty, protection, and guarantee are beyond explanation. When Allah is with them, and He is seeing and hearing everything, and He Himself is sending them on a mission, what fear is there even if the entire creation becomes the enemy!

"So go to him and say, 'Indeed, we are messengers of your Lord, so send with us the Children of Israel and do not torment them. We have come to you with a sign from your Lord. And peace will be upon he who follows the guidance.'"

Although the status of messenger belonged only to Moses, and Aaron was a prophet supporting him, here, by way of general application (Taghlib), the word "rasoola" (two messengers) is used for both.

"so send with us the Children of Israel and do not torment them" — the explanation of this verse has passed under Surah Al-A'raf, verses 104-105. We explained in detail there where Moses wished to take the Children of Israel, and refuted the view of those who try to characterize Moses's call as a nationalist freedom movement.

**The Meaning of "Sign":**

"We have come to you with a sign from your Lord" — "Ayah" here means proof and argument, regardless of the number. The same subject is expressed in Surah Al-A'raf as: "I have come to you with a clear proof from your Lord." We have discussed the question of why Moses was granted miracles in the very first phase, and what the circumstances were that led him to declare them in his very first step.

**Well-Wishing and Warning Together:**

"And peace will be upon he who follows the guidance" — within this phrase lies both the well-wishing and sympathy that characterizes the prophets' call—which Moses was instructed with in verse 44—and also a subtle warning: that safety and peace are only for those who follow God's guidance.
