The meaning is: "Whatever you did until now, you did; but now that God's guidance has reached you through His messengers, the path of safety is to accept it directly, or be prepared to face the consequences."

"Indeed, it has been revealed to us that the punishment will be upon he who denies and turns away.'"

This opens up the underlying meaning of the previous verse in a very gentle manner: "We have received this revelation that punishment comes upon those who deny and turn away."

**Some Delicate Aspects of Verse 48:**

If we reflect on this verse, several delicate aspects emerge:

First, Moses and Aaron did not, like common leaders, try to threaten Pharaoh with a punishment of their own; rather, they merely informed him of the revelation they had received from Allah: that punishment comes upon those who deny. They adopted this method to avoid causing any unnecessary provocation to Pharaoh.

Second, they did not address Pharaoh directly, saying: "If you deny and turn away, punishment will descend upon you"; rather, they stated it in a general form: "whoever does so, his end will be this," so that the message would reach Pharaoh's ears without directly bruising his ego.

Third, the object of "kadhaba" (denied) and the connection of "tawalla" (turned away) are omitted here. They did not say: "whoever denies our messengership and turns away from the guidance we brought"; this was because it was self-evident, and expressing it directly would also wound Pharaoh's pride.

The instruction of gentleness in call given to Moses and Aaron in verse 44 was thus demonstrated: "Deliver the call in such a manner that the message is conveyed, without creating any cause for provocation from your side."

" [Pharaoh] said, 'So who is the Lord of you two, O Moses?'"

**Pharaoh's Challenge:**

Contextual clues show that based on the need for clarity, the text has omitted the intermediate detail: that Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh with the message, and delivered it to him precisely in accordance with the divine instructions. Upon hearing this, Pharaoh said: "So who is the Lord of you two, O Moses?" — meaning: "You have come as messengers of a Lord, but who is this Lord? The lord here is I, so in my court, whose messenger have you come as?"

We explained in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf that Pharaoh was regarded in Egypt as a "God King". In the Egyptian creed, he was the manifestation of their supreme deity—the sun—and under this creed, his statues and idols were worshipped throughout Egypt.

This question of Pharaoh was asked merely in a tone of sarcasm and contempt; despite his claim to divinity, he was not unfamiliar with the fact that the Children of Israel did not accept him as God, though they were his slaves. But until now, the status of the Children of Israel's God in Egypt was that of an inactive God, whose name they might have taken in their homes, but Who had no connection with the practical matters of life. In practical matters, the rule was Pharaoh's alone. This was the first time Moses and Aaron presented God before Pharaoh as an active, living God, and not only presented Him, but placed several major demands before him in His name, along with the warning: "If you want safety, accept our demands, or be prepared to face the consequences." Obviously, Pharaoh was unfamiliar with this active God of the Children of Israel, which is why this voice appeared strange to him, and he asked this question in a tone of surprise and contempt. The passage in the Torah regarding this occasion also supports this: 

"And Pharaoh said, Who is the Lord, that I should obey his voice to let Israel go? I know not the Lord, neither will I let Israel go." (Exodus 5:2)

"He said, 'Our Lord is He Who gave to each thing its creation and then guided [it].'"

**A Brief but Crushing Answer:**

Moses replied: "Our Lord is He Who gave to each thing its creation, and then guided it in accordance with that creation." The explanation of this summary is that every object has a specific form and structure according to its purpose of existence, and then possesses an intuitive guidance within itself to achieve and complete that purpose of existence. The bee was created by the Creator for a specific purpose, and for its completion, He placed within its tiny body all those powers and capabilities that are necessary for it, and then inspired its instinct with how to use those capabilities to gather honey. This is the case with every small and large object in this universe. An ant, a locust, a piece of wood, a sheep, a lion—all have been granted a specific type of creation and an intuitive guidance according to their needs. God has inspired all of them with how to find their food, how to maintain their lineage, how to protect themselves, what to avoid, what to choose, and how to perform their duty in the collective service of this universe.

*The nightingale's song, the parrot's speech, and the cuckoo's call—whose inspiration is it?* Obviously, their Creator's! And that Creator is the Lord of all!

**The Flow of Guidance Upon Everything:**

This favor of power is not limited to animate objects alone; rather, the plants of the garden that bear fruit and flowers, the lily, violet, rose, cypress, and pine that beautify the garden, the apple, pomegranate, and grape that invite desire—whose creation and whose instinct is this? Who can claim the creation of even one of these things, or say that it is his work that placed the quality of an apple within an apple, and of a thorn within a thorn? Who placed these distinct temperaments, distinct colors, distinct fragrances, and distinct benefits and harms within each plant of the garden, each grass of the field, and each shrub of the forest? He Who did is the Lord, not any arrogant mortal who wears a crown and claims divinity.

If we look at the heavens: this sun, this moon, this galaxy, this rainbow, this sky, this cloud, this wind—who created them, and who inspired them with their duties? He Who created them and inspired them with their duties is the Lord. To sit on a throne claiming to be the sun's manifestation is very easy, but who can bring out the sun even one minute before its time, or make it disappear from the horizon!

Within this creation, the status of the noble creature belongs to human. He has been granted the best creation and the highest capabilities, but no one has any share in the creation of their own body or capabilities. It is God Who gave us hands, feet, nose, ears, and eyes, and He Who gave us instinct, nature, intellect, and consciousness. It is through these favors that land and sea, rivers and mountains are all within our reach. We cross the oceans and fly in the air; our reach is not only to the far corners of the earth, but to the moon and Mars. Electricity and atoms are under our control. But let there not be the slightest misunderstanding that this is the work of your own created capabilities. One who is in this misunderstanding is foolish! This is the work of the intellect granted by God, through which human discovers some of His laws and reaches the moon and Mars. If through the intellect granted by God he discovers some of His laws and benefits from them, he should not become so arrogant as to challenge God's divinity.

Moses answered in very brief words, but within them is enclosed a massive detail; and though the words are gentle, the answer is crushing. In highly comprehensive words, he clarified to Pharaoh that the right to be Lord belongs only to Him Who created and guided; you have no share in creation or guidance, so how did you claim to be Lord! The same truth is expressed in Surah Al-A'la as: "Exalt the name of your Lord, the Most High, Who created and proportioned, and Who destined and [then] guided." (87:1-3)

In reality, to establish God's proof against human, his own nature and intellect are sufficient. It is Allah's additional favor that He arranged for human's guidance through His prophets, messengers, books, and scriptures. "Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness, even if he presents his excuses." (Al-Qiyamah: 14-15)

" [Pharaoh] said, 'Then what is the case of the former generations?'"

**Pharaoh's Sarcasm:**

Instead of seriously considering Moses's reminding, Pharaoh asked in a tone of sarcasm and contempt: "Very well, if you have come from the Lord, tell us: what is the state of the former generations, and what happened to them?" The purpose was: "Since you are coming from there, you must be aware of all the details; tell us their state!" It must be noted that arrogant and conceited people always raise such irrelevant questions to dilute serious discussions and maintain their hold over their blind followers.

" He said, 'The knowledge thereof is with my Lord in a Record. My Lord neither errs nor forgets.'"

**A Dignified Answer and Reminding:**

Though Pharaoh's question was irrelevant and contemptuous, Moses not only gave a dignified and serious answer, but also delivered another highly wise lesson of reminding and warning. He said: "The record of those nations is preserved with my Lord in a Great Record." Contextual clues show that the indefiniteness (tankir) of the word "Kitab" (Record) here points to its grandeur: "Let no one assume this is an ordinary book; rather, within it is recorded every single detail." When it is opened, everyone will cry out: "Oh, woe to us! What is this book that leaves nothing small or great except that it has enumerated it?" (Al-Kahf: 49)

"My Lord neither errs nor forgets" — meaning, my Lord's knowledge is entirely free of error and forgetfulness. It is neither possible for anything to remain outside His knowledge, nor for it to enter His knowledge incorrectly, nor for Him to forget anything that has entered His knowledge.

Reflect upon this: Moses could have answered this irrelevant and sarcastic question by saying: "Do not ask me useless questions; I have come as a messenger of God, I have not claimed to know the unseen, nor that the entire record of the past nations is with me." But instead of this, he pointed out in a highly eloquent manner that: "Though I do not know all the unseen, my Lord knows everything, and His knowledge is free of error and forgetfulness; the entire record is preserved with Him." For the nations that have vanished from the earth, let no one remain in the misunderstanding that they are now merely tales of the past; rather, the deeds of all are recorded in God's register. Obviously, when every single thing is preserved, it is not preserved merely for the sake of preservation, but for accountability, reward, and punishment. Since this was entirely obvious, Moses did not express it in words, but his manner of speech expressed it beautifully. On some occasions, a hint is far more effective than explicit statement.

" [He] Who has made for you the earth as a bed and inserted for you therein pathways and sent down from the sky, water and produced thereby categories of various plants. Eat and pasture your livestock. Indeed, in that are signs for those of intelligence. From the earth We created you, and into it We will return you, and from it We will extract you another time. And We certainly showed Pharaoh Our signs - all of them - but he denied and refused."

**A Parenthetical Insertion:**

Contextual clues show that these four verses are not part of Moses's speech, but are parenthetical statements directly from Allah. This parenthetical insertion is highly eloquent. Its benefit is that the discourse does not remain merely a tale of the past, but is applied fully to the present. Alongside the answer Moses gave to Pharaoh, Allah joined His own words to address the chiefs of the Quraysh: "This is not merely a tale of the past, but a narrative of your own self. Listen to who your Lord is, and who is truly worthy of your worship and obedience."

**Proof of God's Lordship from His Provision:**

This is proof of God's Lordship from His provision: "The One Who made the earth as a cradle for you, made high mountains in it, and carved pathways and passes through them for you to travel. He sent down water from the sky, and produced thereby distinct categories of plants and trees, each of which invites through the tongue of its state: eat, benefit, and pasture your livestock." Indeed, the One Who made all these arrangements is worthy of being accepted as Lord. It is stated that within this system of provision, there are many signs for people of intelligence. The signs of the Creator's power, wisdom, and mercy in this system are not hidden from those who possess intelligence and use it. This system of provision is a witness both to God's monotheism and to the arrival of a Day of Account.

**Deriving the Resurrection from Provision:**

This derives the Resurrection from this system of provision: "We created you from this very earth, and into it We will return you after death, and from it We will extract you another time." The meaning is: "The demand of this system of provision is that after this, a Day should arrive when the justice of the Lord of this universe manifests. Those who respected God's blessings should receive their reward, and those who used these blessings to spread corruption on God's earth should face their punishment." It is stated that recreating you is not difficult for Us. "Since We created you from this very earth initially and faced no difficulty, why should it be difficult to recreate you from it, specifically when We are returning you to it after death?"

"And We certainly showed Pharaoh Our signs - all of them - but he denied and refused" — that is, We showed Pharaoh all those signs that manifested in Egypt one after another to warn him, but he persisted in his denial. The details of these signs are in the Torah, and we have referred to them in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf.

If you observe these verses, you will find that Allah has used first-person plural pronouns (We, Our) throughout, which is why these are parenthetical statements from Him, not part of Moses's speech.

"He said, 'Have you come to us to drive us out of our land with your magic, O Moses?'"

**Pharaoh's Political Move:**

We explained in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf that the greatest fear Pharaoh and his chiefs had regarding the Children of Israel was that their number was continuously increasing. They feared that if they became powerful, they might align with foreign enemies and drive Pharaoh and his nation out of the land. It was to escape this danger that the scheme of killing Israeli children was run, but it failed. The Children of Israel continued to increase, though they lacked a leader to organize them. Pharaoh feared Moses even before his journey to Midian, because Moses was always standing as a shield to defend his oppressed kinsmen. Consequently, his entry into the city was restricted, and he ultimately had to leave for Midian due to the killing of the Egyptian. Now when he returned, and returned with such majesty that he placed several major demands before Pharaoh, Pharaoh perceived that the danger he feared had manifested in reality. Consequently, he said to Moses: "O Moses, have you come to us to drive us out of our land with your magic?" 

Pharaoh was a highly shrewd politician. He sought to incite his nation by characterizing Moses's call as a political danger, and sought to dilute the impact of his miracles by calling them magic.

" 'Then we will surely bring you magic like it, so make between us and you an appointment, which we will not fail to keep and neither will you, in a place that is midway.'"

"Maw'id" is used both for the time of appointment and the place of appointment. In this verse, it can accommodate both meanings, and in the subsequent verse, it is used for the time of appointment.

"Suwa" means middle or equal. "Mahanan suwa" means a place that is equal and midway for both sides, where our people and your people can gather easily.

Pharaoh said to Moses: "If you have risen to drive us out of our land with your magic, we do not lack magic; we can bring magic like it. So make an appointment between us and you at a place midway, where people from both sides can gather at a designated time, and neither you nor we will fail to keep it." Pharaoh's scheme was to have the contest in an open field before the entire public, so they could defeat Moses before everyone and destroy his credibility in the very first phase.

" [Moses] said, 'Your appointment is the day of the festival and let the people be assembled at midmorning.'"

**Accepting the Challenge:**

Since Moses was entirely certain of his success—Allah had reassured him: "You will be successful and victorious in every field"—he accepted Pharaoh's challenge without hesitation. The Egyptians had two or three major festivals during the year. He designated one of those festival days, and also designated the time: "let the people be assembled at midmorning." A festival day is a day of leisure for the people, and midmorning is considered the most suitable time for a public gathering. Moses also willed to shatter the illusion of Pharaoh's magic before everyone.

"So Pharaoh went away and put together his plan, then he came [to the contest]."

**Pharaoh's Preparations:**

"Fatwalla" (so he went away) — after making the agreement with Moses, Pharaoh went away and occupied himself with gathering his plans, and then came to the field of contest with full preparations. The text is brief here, which is why it does not detail his plans, but other places in the Qur'an specify that he consulted his chiefs, and they advised him: "There is a danger of defeat if we compete with ordinary magicians, so the wise course is to send emissaries to all parts of the kingdom to gather all expert magicians." Consequently, this advice was acted upon; emissaries were sent, and all major magicians were gathered.

"Moses said to them, 'Woe to you! Do not invent a lie against Allah lest He destroy you with a punishment. And he who invents a lie has failed.'"

**Moses's Speech in the Field of Contest:**

This is the speech Moses delivered first before the gathered assembly. Addressing Pharaoh, his people, and the magicians, he warned: "Woe to you! You have come to oppose the truth in defense of falsehood. Do not invent a lie against Allah, lest He destroy you with a punishment. Remember that those who invent a lie against Allah fail."

Inventing a lie against Allah, as we have explained in various places, refers to shirk. The reasons for characterizing shirk as inventing a lie against Allah were discussed under Surah Al-Ma'idah and Al-An'am. In the preceding verses, you read that the debate with Pharaoh was regarding his claim to be Lord, and Moses had refuted this claim, stating that the Lord is He Who created and guided, not you. Now, Moses delivered the same truth to all of Pharaoh's supporters: "God has no partner; if you associate partners with Him of your own accord, you invent a lie against Allah. You have no authority from Him for this, and you deserve that God destroy you with a punishment. Those who invent a lie against Allah ultimately fail."

"So they disputed over their affair among themselves and kept secret their private conversation."

**The Secret Meeting of Pharaoh's Chiefs:**

"Tanazu' fil-hadith" and "tanazu' al-amr" are used in Arabic for mutual consultation and exchange of views. That is, when all the chiefs of the government and the magicians gathered, they held a secret meeting. The subsequent verses show that in this meeting, Pharaoh's side sought to convince the magicians that Moses and Aaron were expert magicians whose objective was to organize the Children of Israel with their magic, drive us out of the land, and destroy the civilization we have built; therefore, this is not a time for negligence, but to oppose this danger with united strength, and remember that whoever wins today will be successful.

"They said, 'Indeed, these two are magicians who want to drive you out of your land with their magic and do away with your exemplary way.'"

"In" here is in the sense of "inna" (indeed), and the "lam" that follows is the clue to this.

"Muthla" is the feminine of "amthal", meaning highest, best, and exemplary.

This is the statement spoken in the secret meeting by Pharaoh and his chiefs to incite the magicians against Moses and Aaron.

First, to incite professional rivalry within the magicians, they said: "These two are expert magicians." The purpose was: "They have come with the same weapon you possess; so if you display weakness against them, your own credibility will be destroyed."

Second, they explained: "They want to awe us with their magic, organize the Children of Israel, and drive you out of your land."

Third, they said: "Your exemplary civilization and way of life, which you have built with such hard labor, is in grave danger. If Moses and Aaron succeed, they will destroy this exemplary civilization completely."

Reflect upon these statements: those in power always use similar weapons against the people of truth, just as Pharaoh and his chiefs sought to do against Moses and Aaron.

Note also that the phrases "your land" and "your civilization" were used merely to deceive the public; in the Pharaoh's system, other than a specific aristocracy, the status of others was merely that of slaves and laborers. But when those in power want to use the public for their own purposes, they deceive them in this very manner.

" 'So resolve upon your plan and then come in line. And he who surpasses today will have succeeded.'"

This is the advice given to the magicians after consultation: "Since the stage is critical and the contest is with expert magicians, all magicians must unite their strength and present a united front." This advice was necessary because, just as there are rivalries among political parties, with each working only for its own interest, there is rivalry among professional groups, with each wanting to surpass the other. Pharaoh and his chiefs feared that if there was such division among the magicians, they would face defeat, which is why they advised them to present a united front instead of competing individually.

"And he who surpasses today will have succeeded" — this points to the critical nature of the day: "Today is the decisive day. If we win, the future success is ours; and if we lose, then all those dangers will inevitably materialize which are feared in the case of Moses and Aaron's success."

"They said, 'O Moses, either you throw or we will be the first to throw.'"

**The Truth and Falsehood in the Field of Contest:**

After this consultation, the magicians and Moses entered the field of contest with their respective groups. Standing face to face, the magicians, in accordance with their professional etiquette, said to Moses: "Either you throw your staff first, or we throw ours." From their statement, it follows that though they gave the option to Moses, they expressed their own desire to throw first. It must be noted that in such contests, priority (who goes first) holds great importance. Each side desires to spellbind the audience with their first act, so that the opponent's courage is broken.

"He said, 'Rather, you throw.' And suddenly their ropes and staffs seemed to him from their magic that they were moving swiftly."

**The Magicians' Act:**

Since Moses was entirely certain of his success—Allah had reassured him of victory—he did not initiate, but gave the option to them first: "Rather, you throw." They were already prepared, so as soon as Moses spoke, they threw their ropes and staffs upon the ground, and through their magic, it appeared to Moses that they were moving swiftly. Since the magicians knew that Moses turned his staff into a snake, they tried to defeat him with similar magic, adding to it by making their ropes also appear like snakes. In their own view, they had made full arrangements to defeat Moses, because Moses turned only one staff into a snake, while they turned many staffs and ropes into snakes. But all of this, as is clear from the word "yukhayyalu" (seemed/imagined), was merely the effect of magic upon the audience's imagination. Magic does not alter the true nature of an object; it merely affects the observer's mind and imagination, and if the reality appears, the illusion of magic is immediately shattered.

"And Moses felt apprehension within himself."

**Moses Felt Apprehension:**

Upon seeing this sight, Moses naturally felt some apprehension, because until now, the true power of his own staff had not manifested. He realized that the contest was indeed severe. Though he was reassured of victory by Allah's promise, when a situation appears more complex than expected, a person experiences a temporary apprehension. Such a temporary apprehension was felt by Moses.

"We said, 'Fear not. Indeed, it is you who are the superior.'"

**Allah Almighty's Reassurance:**

To remove Moses's apprehension, this glad tiding was immediately sent down: "Do not fear in the least. No matter how great their magic is, you will be victorious and superior."

"And throw what is in your right hand; it will swallow up what they have crafted. What they have crafted is but the trick of a magician, and the magician will not succeed wherever he goes."

**The Difference Between Miracles and Magic:**

Along with this, the instruction came: "Throw what is in your right hand; it will swallow up their entire illusion." What they have displayed is merely a magician's trick, and a magician does not succeed wherever he goes. Though the primary purpose here is to show that a magician's magic does not stand against the truth, the statement is made as a general rule: "a magician never succeeds," because this is the reality. Magic depends entirely on illusion and the observer's imagination; it has no effect on reality. Furthermore, those in this profession always belong to the lower strata of society, who can fool some simple people for a few days with their tricks, but have no role in building or reforming society. In contrast, the miracles that manifest through the prophets carry a majesty, and they manifest through those who are the choice of humanity, whose enemies also bear witness to their pure character, and who carry a great call whose truth is recognized by the hearts of their opponents. When these people depart from the world, they change the course of history. From their call, a new chapter of history begins, and the world is reborn. Is there any magician whose minor contribution to any field of human life is known? 

Keep also in mind here that our theologians have tried to differentiate between miracles and magic through logic, which is a futile attempt! The difference between a false light and the sun is not determined through logic! The observers determine it themselves, provided they possess eyes to see.

"So the magicians fell down in prostration. They said, 'We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses.'"

In accordance with Allah's command, Moses threw his staff upon the ground, and it became a great serpent, swallowing up all the snakes and ropes of the magicians. When the magicians saw this sight, their eyes were opened. A magician, if he retains any spark of recognizing the truth, can distinguish between his own trick and a prophet's miracle better than anyone else. These magicians retained a spark of recognizing the truth. Verse 73 also shows that these magicians already realized that what Moses was presenting was not magic, which is why they were hesitant to compete with him; but the government's agents forced them by raising political dangers. They came to compete under compulsion, but when they saw the miracle of Moses against their trick, their eyes were opened, and they fell down in prostration. Falling down in prostration during that era was a way of recognizing someone's superiority in art, but these magicians did not stop at this; they declared before the entire assembly: "We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses." Imagine the effect of this confession and declaration of faith by the magicians before the entire public upon Pharaoh and his chiefs! But Pharaoh was a shrewd politician; he tried to handle the situation with great cunning, and immediately raised a new issue to deceive the public.

" [Pharaoh] said, 'You believed him before I gave you permission. Indeed, he is your leader who has taught you magic. So I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides, and I will surely crucify you on the trunks of date-palms, and you will surely know which of us is more severe in [giving] punishment and more enduring.'"

**Pharaoh's Political Deception:**

Pharaoh said to the magicians: "You believed Moses without my permission. Now I understand: in reality, he is your teacher who taught you magic, and through a mutual conspiracy, you have staged this drama of victory and defeat to undermine the government and pave the way for Moses's rule. This is a conspiracy against the state, and I will give you the punishment given to rebels. I will cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides, and then crucify you on the trunks of date-palms. At that time, you will realize whose punishment is more severe and more enduring—mine or your teacher's."

From the magicians' statement, it is clear that they did not merely recognize Moses as a truthful man, but declared: "We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses." Within this declaration was implied the rejection of Pharaoh's divinity, which struck at both Pharaoh's divinity and his rule, which is why this was not merely a matter of faith, but a challenge to his authority. How could he tolerate this in his kingdom!

"Indeed, he is your leader who has taught you magic" — to deceive the public, Pharaoh raised the issue: "In reality, Moses is your teacher who taught you this art. You had a conspiracy. You decided that you would accept defeat before your teacher in this manner, so that his awe would be established over the people, and then you would succeed in your conspiracy against the state." Though this was completely absurd—Pharaoh had lost with his own army in his own selected field—he had to make up some story to dilute the impact of the defeat, so he did; and credit must be given to his political shrewdness, and what wonder if he succeeded in deceiving many! But truth cannot be suppressed by such deceptive statements.

"So I will surely cut off..." — when the magicians were declared guilty of conspiracy, the punishment given to rebels during that era was announced for them: cutting off their hands and feet on opposite sides and crucifying them publicly, so others might be warned. The mention of "on the trunks of date-palms" points to its being public.

**An Illusion of the Magicians:**

"and you will surely know which of us is more severe in punishment and more enduring" — that is, you assume that if you act against your teacher's will, he will place you through some major trouble with his magic, which is why you have become his tools. But when you taste my punishment, you will realize that my punishment is far more severe and enduring than his. It must be remembered that there exists an illusion among magicians that if they act against their teacher's will, the teacher will place them through some trouble with his magic. Pharaoh exploited this illusion to warn the magicians that my punishment would be far more severe.

"They said, 'Never will we prefer you over what has come to us of clear proofs and [over] Him Who created us. So decree whatever you are to decree. You can only decree for this worldly life.'"

**The Wonder of Faith:**

Pharaoh delivered a very severe threat, expecting to intimidate the magicians, but he was now confronting not professional magicians, but firm believers. They replied to Pharaoh without hesitation: "We will never prefer you over those clear proofs that have come to our observation, and over our Creator Who brought us into existence. Decree whatever you wish to decree; you can only decree for this worldly life, and we do not care for it." 

Observe the wonder of faith! These are the very magicians who, when called to the contest, had pleaded to Pharaoh for a reward if they won; now, the light of faith had so illumined their hearts that nothing of this world held any value in their eyes, to the extent that they were fully prepared to sacrifice their lives on the path of faith.

"Indeed, we have believed in our Lord that He may forgive us our sins and what you compelled us of magic. And Allah is better and more enduring.'"

They added: "We believe in our Lord in the hope that He will forgive us our past errors and that magic which you compelled us to perform, and Allah is better and more enduring."

From "and what you compelled us of magic", it is clear that these people were not willing to compete with Moses. They knew that what Moses was presenting was not magic, but something else; but they were forced to compete by the government. Ultimately, Allah clarified the reality to them, and their breasts were expanded to accept and declare it.

"And Allah is better and more enduring" — this is a direct and complete answer to Pharaoh's statement: "and you will surely know which of us is more severe in punishment and more enduring." They said: "You threaten us with your punishment, but we have chosen our Lord, Who is better than everything and is immortal."

"Indeed, he who comes to his Lord as a criminal - indeed, for him is Hell; he will neither die therein nor live."

**A parenthetical Insertion:**

This and the subsequent two verses, in my view, are parenthetical statements from Allah. Just as in verses 53-56, Allah joined His own words to Moses's speech to make it complete and applicable, He joined His words to the magicians' response to make it complete. The reason for taking these verses as parenthesis is that the manner in which reward, punishment, and the Hereafter are described in them is fitting for Allah Almighty, and their warning is distinct from the previous verses. It is stated: "Whoever presents himself before his Lord as a criminal—for him is Hell, in which he will neither die nor live." "He will neither die therein nor live" — this is such an expression of the severity, eternity, and helplessness of the punishment that it is impossible to explain it in words. No matter how great a misfortune in this world, death is ultimately the final escape; but where there is no death, and the misfortunes of death are surrounding one from all sides, where can one escape.

"But he who comes to Him as a believer having done righteous deeds - for those will be the highest degrees."

This describes the successful group in contrast: "As for those who present themselves before Him as believers, having done righteous deeds—for them are the high degrees."

**Righteous Deeds are a Condition for the Acceptance of Faith:**

If we reflect on "as a believer having done righteous deeds", it is clear that before Allah, the only credible faith is that which is accompanied by righteous deeds. Faith that is devoid of righteous deeds holds no value before Allah. Such faith is like a dry tree that is devoid of leaves and fruit. A tree that did not produce its leaves and fruit in this world, how shall it bear fruit in the Hereafter! Just as faith is a condition for the acceptance of actions, righteous deeds are a condition for the acceptance of faith.

"Gardens of perpetual residence beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally. And that is the reward of him who purifies himself."

**Paradise is Not a Cheap Thing:**

This details the high degrees: "the gardens of eternity beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever." It is stated: "and that is the reward of him who purifies himself." The meaning is: "This is not something to be obtained through vain desires; rather, only those will be worthy of it who strive to purify their souls from every taint of false concepts and bad actions." The slips a person makes on this path have been provided with means of rectification by Allah in His Book, and the concessions human requires due to his weakness have also been granted. To deny these concessions is excess (ghuluw) in religion, but it should also be noted that those who have minimized the importance of actions and opened the doors of indulgence—whether our ancient or modern theologians—have indeed made Paradise a very cheap thing, which provides great comfort to the negligent, but there is no room for this theory in the Qur'an. The key to Paradise is in the hand of Allah; He has not handed it over to these theologians, which is why safety lies in remaining safe from such false desires. The Qur'an has counted them among the vain desires (Amani) of the Jews, the details of which passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah.

"And We inspired to Moses, 'Travel by night with My servants and strike for them a dry path in the sea; you will not fear being overtaken [by Pharaoh] nor be afraid [of drowning].'"

**The Meaning of "Fadhrib" (Strike):**

"Dharb-e-tareeq" in Arabic idioms means to make a path, but within its use here lies a subtle reference to Moses's staff, because the staff had a role in making this path, and the relationship of the staff with the word "dharb" (striking) is obvious.

**An Example of Omission:**

In "you will not fear being overtaken nor be afraid", the object of "takhsha" (fear) is omitted based on the principle of contrast. If we expand this omission, the complete statement would be: "you will not fear being overtaken [by Pharaoh] nor fear being drowned." I have maintained this in the translation.

**The Command of Migration to Moses:**

Here, a massive portion of the conflict between Moses and Pharaoh is omitted, which we have discussed in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf. When Moses demanded of Pharaoh to let the Children of Israel go with him to celebrate a festival of sacrifice, Pharaoh not only rejected this demand, but increased the oppression and violence being committed against the Children of Israel. During this time, Allah Almighty sent various plagues upon Egypt one after another to warn Pharaoh; when a plague came, he would plead to Moses: "If this plague is removed through your prayer, I will let the Children of Israel go with you." But when the plague was removed through Moses's prayer and Allah's mercy, his rebellion would return, and he would break his covenant. Several years passed in this conflict. During this time, Moses fully discharged his duty of delivering the message to Pharaoh and his nation. Ultimately, when the proof was established against Pharaoh and his nation, Moses was commanded by Allah to migrate with the Children of Israel.

Until now, Moses's demand, as we explained in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf, was only to be allowed to go with the Children of Israel for a three-day journey into the wilderness for sacrifice; but now, since the proof was established against Pharaoh and his nation, they were commanded to migrate from the land in accordance with the divine way. The command came: "Travel by night with My servants." "My servants" refers to those who believed in Allah. The vast majority of them were the Children of Israel, but a small group among them consisted of those Egyptians who had believed; the magicians whose faith was mentioned earlier were among the Egyptians, not the Israelites. "My servants" includes all those who believed in Moses, whether Egyptians or Israelites. A believing man of the family of Pharaoh is also specifically mentioned in the Qur'an.

**The Wonder of Divine Power:**

"and strike for them a dry path in the sea" — "sea" here refers to the northern gulf of the Red Sea. The instruction was: "Reach the sea coast with the entire group, and strike the sea with your staff to make a dry path for the people." This style of expression shows the authority Allah granted to Moses through his miracle: "If you command the sea with its gesture, it will make a dry path for you." The definite article on "Al-Bahr" (the sea) indicates that the place where they had to reach was specifically designated for Moses. The details of this event in the Torah are presented here to show how the divine power manifested. Exodus 14 states:

"And the Lord said unto Moses, Wherefore criest thou unto me? speak unto the Children of Israel, that they go forward: But lift thou up thy rod, and stretch out thine hand over the sea, and divide it: and the Children of Israel shall go on dry ground through the midst of the sea... And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea; and the Lord caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind all that night, and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided. And the Children of Israel went into the midst of the sea upon the dry ground: and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left. And the Egyptians pursued..." (14:15-23)

This shows that this miracle manifested through the operation of an east wind. Through it, the water of the gulf was divided in such a manner that a dry path was made for the Children of Israel.

The Drowning of Pharaoh and His Army
'And there covered them from the sea that which covered them' (Fa-ghashiyahum mina al-yammi ma ghashiyahum). In Arabic, this style of expression is used to depict such dreadfulness or grandeur of a thing that words fall short of expressing it.
It is known from the Torah that although Pharaoh, fed up with the miracles of Moses, had allowed them to leave, his mind changed after their departure, and he pursued them with his army, ultimately being engulfed along with his army by the relentless waves of the sea. Its details in the Torah are as follows:
'When the king of Egypt was told that the people had fled, Pharaoh and his servants had a change of heart toward the people, and they said, 'What is this we have done, letting Israel go from serving us?' So he made ready his chariot and took his people with him. He took six hundred chosen chariots, even all the chariots of Egypt, with officers over all of them.' Exodus Chapter 14:5-7
After this, the detail of their drowning is described as follows:
'Then the Lord said to Moses, 'Stretch out your hand over the sea, so that the water may come back over the Egyptians, over their chariots, and over their horsemen.' So Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and at dawn the sea returned to its normal state, and the Egyptians fled into it; and the Lord swept the Egyptians into the midst of the sea. The waters returned and covered the chariots and the horsemen, even Pharaoh’s entire army that had gone into the sea after them; not even one of them remained. But the sons of Israel walked on dry land through the midst of the sea, and the waters were like a wall to them on their right hand and on their left.' Exodus Chapter 14:26-29

The True Responsibility of a Leader
This is an expression of regret over Pharaoh and his destructive leadership, as he drowned his nation in the sea of destruction due to his misleading ways and did not guide them rightly. The phrase 'And he did not guide' (wa ma hada) points to the reality that the primary responsibility of every leader and ruler is to guide their nation correctly. His status is indeed that of a shepherd, whom Allah assigns to herd and protect a flock of His creation. Wretched is that shepherd who sacrifices the entire flock for his own ego.

Verses 80-82
Admonition to the Children of Israel
After crossing the river, Allah reminded the Children of Israel of His great favors, and reminded them of the immense responsibility that lay upon them as a right of those favors. At the same time, He warned them of the fate destined for those who show ingratitude for these blessings and choose the path of disbelief and transgression instead of gratitude. In the exact same context, with minor variations in wording, this subject matter has already appeared in Surah Al-Baqarah. We have explained every aspect of it in detail there, which is why we will limit ourselves to brief indications here. Those who seek details may look at what has been presented under verses 46-57 in the commentary of Al-Baqarah.
'And We made a covenant with you on the right side of the Mount' (Wa wa'adnakum janiba at-turi al-ayman) refers to the promise that Allah made with the Children of Israel through Moses to grant them the Torah, and for which He summoned them to that sacred side of Mount Tur where Moses was granted prophethood upon his return from Madyan... The manner in which the Children of Israel valued this blessing of Allah will be detailed ahead.
'And We sent down upon you manna and quails' (Wa nazzalna 'alaykumu al-manna wa as-salwa). Manna and quail have been explained under verse 57 of Al-Baqarah. The degree to which the Children of Israel appreciated this blessing has also been mentioned before.
'Eat of the good things with which We have provided you, and do not transgress therein' (Kulu min tayyibati ma razaqnakum wa la tatghaw fihi). This states the obligation linked to these blessings. The reminder of this obligation is conveyed implicitly by every blessing itself, and Allah also repeatedly instructed the Children of Israel in words through their prophet. Evidence of this exists in the Torah. The phrasing of 'and do not transgress therein' also implies that the abundance of God's blessings should not lead you into transgression. This transgression is the tradition of Pharaoh, and you have already witnessed his end with your own eyes.
'And he upon whom My wrath descends has fallen' (Wa man yahllil 'alayhi ghadabi faqad hawa), meaning this type of transgression invites the wrath of God, and whoever is struck by God's wrath falls. 'Falls' means from the throne of honor to the floor of humiliation. You have also witnessed this event with your own eyes—what was the end of the one who claimed divinity?
'And indeed, I am the Perpetual Forgiver of whoever repents and believes and does righteousness and then continues in guidance' (Wa inni la-ghaffarun liman taba wa amana wa 'amila salihan thumma ihtada). This shows the path of salvation for those who firmly resolve to act upon these instructions. It states that if any error is committed by such people, the way to rectify it is repentance. They should turn back from their error, renew their faith and good deeds, and seek further guidance from God for the future.

Verses 83-84
A Style of Arabic Grammar
After 'a'jalaka' (made you hasten), the preposition 'min' (from) is a contextual clue indicating that a deleted verb denoting 'leaving behind' should be assumed here. This is because 'an' (about/from) does not align directly with 'a'jalaka', and we have frequently explained this Arabic idiom: when a verb and its preposition lack apparent compatibility, the verb implicitly carries the meaning of another verb that fits the preposition. In light of this principle, the meaning of the verse would be: 'O Moses, what made you hasten away from your people, leaving them behind?'
The Hastening of Moses to Receive the Torah
This mentions the occasion when Allah summoned Moses to receive the Torah at the same place on Mount Tur where prophethood was bestowed upon him. For this, Allah had fixed a period of thirty days. However, out of intense longing, Moses entrusted the care of his people to Aaron and reached Tur somewhat early. This is mentioned in the Torah, though it does not specify any duration or the haste of Moses:
'And the Lord said to Moses, 'Come up to Me on the mountain and stay here, and I will give you the tablets of stone with the law and commandments I have written for their instruction.' Then Moses set out with Joshua his assistant, and Moses went up on the mountain of God. He said to the elders, 'Wait here for us until we come back to you. Aaron and Hur are with you; anyone who has a dispute can go to them.' Exodus Chapter 24:12-14
In Al-A'raf, it is mentioned thus:
'And We appointed for Moses thirty nights and completed them with ten, so the term of his Lord was completed as forty nights. And Moses said to his brother Aaron, 'Take my place among my people, do right, and do not follow the way of the corrupters.' (142 - Al-A'raf)
Moses answered Allah's question by saying: 'O my Lord! My people are closely following in my footsteps, and I have hastened to You, my Lord, before the appointed time, only to please You.'
This haste of Moses, as is clear from his statement, was purely the result of intense longing and the desire to seek Allah's pleasure. From this perspective, it was not an evil but a demand of divine love. However, this very haste became a severe test for him and a harsh trial (fitnah) for his people. The wicked and mischievous elements among his nation took advantage of his absence and led the people into calf-worship, and Aaron, despite his utmost efforts, was unable to control the situation. The temperament of Moses's people at that time was highly impressionable. Their state has been described in Surah Al-A'raf, where immediately after crossing the sea, when they encountered idol-worshipping communities, they demanded of Moses to make an idol for them like those of the other nations. This event occurred just shortly before Moses went to Mount Tur. On that occasion, Moses had rebuked these troublemakers. Moses must have been aware of the corrupters among his people, and he was in the best position to judge the extent to which Aaron could control these wayward people. Under such circumstances, even his absence for a few days could become a trial for the nation. But in the intensity of his longing, he could not fully gauge these aspects, and the consequence emerged in an extremely dangerous form.
We have pointed out in various places that when any lapse is committed by the Prophets, it is never of the nature of following base desires; rather, sometimes they exceed the desired limits in the path of truth and seeking God's pleasure. This in itself is not an evil, but since the Prophets (peace be upon them) are the scale of truth and their every word and action serves as a model for others, Allah takes them to task even for such matters. Consequently, Moses was called to account for this haste. In this Surah, as you read in the introduction, our Prophet is taught patience and waiting; therefore, this incident from the life of Moses is highlighted here to show that in the struggle to establish the religion, the way of patience and waiting is aligned with divine wisdom, otherwise sometimes haste made even with the best of intentions can cause many to stumble.

Allah informed Moses: 'You hastened ahead of your people, leaving them behind to seek Our pleasure, and in your absence We have put your people to trial, and the Samiri has led them astray.'
'We have tried your people' (fatanna qawmaka) refers to the divine sunnah (practice) which we have discussed in detail under the verse 'We tried you with many trials' (wa fatannaka futuna). Whether individuals or nations, righteous or wicked, everyone is tested at all times, and through this trial, Allah distinguishes the pure from the impure. The objective here is to state that We put your nation to trial, and they failed this test; the Samiri led them astray.
The Trial of Samiri
There is no mention of the Samiri in the Torah. Consequently, only as much about him is known as is stated in the Qur'an and what follows. The narrators of the Torah have attributed this entire trial to Aaron. It is a favor of the Qur'an that it identified the real originator of this fitnah and exonerated Aaron from this false accusation.
Was the Samiri an Israelite or was he among those Egyptians who migrated with Moses? This is an irrelevant and useless question. We do not wish to delve into this unnecessary debate. What is known from the Qur'an is that he was an extremely cunning and deceptive hypocrite who had infiltrated the group of Moses purely for his own mischievous purposes. The upcoming verses will reveal that he also knew the art of putting on a show of mystical visions and miracles. Such clever individuals easily trap simple-minded commoners in their web of deception. He took full advantage of Moses's absence. As noted above, Moses arrived early, and then Allah, under His training wisdom, added another ten days to his stay. Thus, the Samiri got a free hand. He gathered around him all those elements of the Children of Israel who already possessed an idolatrous mindset. He convinced them, saying: 'We do not know where Moses has gone in search of God. If you donate your jewelry, I will forge a deity for you that will guide you. Moses left you here and vanished; how long will you wait?' The Samiri was also an expert in the Egyptian art of sculpting. He fashioned a calf from the jewelry people gave him, shaped in such a way that when the wind passed through it, it made a lowing sound. As will be seen later, he claimed this sound was a result of his miracle and succeeded in fooling the simple-minded public. Aaron exerted his full strength to save the people from this trial, but he could not subdue those who were previously suppressed only by Moses's awe, to the point that Aaron's own life was put in danger. Aaron also realized that if he took any further steps, it would cause more harm than good, and the unity of the community would be completely shattered; therefore, he deemed it best to wait for Moses's return.
The Return of Moses and His Wrath upon the Nation
When Moses was informed of this incident, the reaction was exactly what was natural. He returned to his people in a state of intense anger and sorrow. His anger was directed at the successful mischief of the troublemakers who ruined all his hard work, and as the upcoming verses show, it was also directed at Aaron due to the lack of clear details, feeling that Aaron failed to uphold the instruction to remain vigilant against the corrupters. His sorrow was over the wretchedness of his nation—that he had gone to receive Allah's book for them, and they had engaged in worshipping a calf!
First of all, he rebuked the nation: 'O foolish ones, what have you done! Did not God make a most gracious promise to you to give you the Book and Shariah? Did the fulfillment of this promise take so long that you could not remain patient? Or did you wish that the wrath of your Lord should descend upon you, so you broke the covenant I made with you?' This covenant refers to the instruction we mentioned earlier: upon leaving for Mount Tur, Moses had instructed all the elders of the nation that he was going to receive the Shariah, and until his return, the people must stay put and follow the guidance of Aaron in all matters.

Because of the conciseness (ijaz) of this verse, our commentators have faced great perplexity in its interpretation. We will first explain its meaning in our own words and then clarify its individual terms.
The Excuse of the Nation's Elders
As we indicated above, Moses first rebuked those elders of the nation upon whom he had placed the responsibility of looking after the people in his absence. These people expressed their helplessness and constraint, saying that what happened had nothing to do with their intention or will; rather, they simply threw off the burden of the people's jewelry that had been entrusted to them upon the people's demand, and thus the Samiri cast it to fashion the calf. That is, this crime lies entirely upon the Samiri and those commoners who fell for his deception and whose jewelry was in our custody. We were forced under their pressure to hand over their jewelry to them, and with that jewelry, the Samiri created this fitnah.
'We did not break our promise to you of our own accord' (Ma akhlafna maw'idaka bimalkina). 'Malk' means choice and authority. Meaning: 'We tried our utmost within our capability to stand by the covenant we made with you, but the situation got so out of hand that it became impossible for us to control the masses.'
'But we were made to carry burdens of the ornament of the people' (Wa lakinna hummilna awzaran min zinati al-qawm). 'Zinat-ul-qawm' refers to the ornaments of the people that were entrusted to these leaders. It appears that to keep their valuable jewelry safe from the danger of loss during the rush of travel, many people had left their jewelry in the custody of these leaders, and they had reluctantly taken this responsibility upon themselves as a service. Was this jewelry belonging to the Children of Israel or the Copts? The biblical accounts on this matter are contradictory. Chapter 35 of the Book of Exodus suggests it belonged to the Israelites, but another account in the same book suggests that at the time of leaving the country, the Israelites had borrowed many gold and silver ornaments and vessels from the Egyptians. However, this second account seems completely against reason. Firstly, the Israelites in Egypt did not hold such a status that they could borrow gold and silver jewelry from the Egyptians, nor would the Egyptians hand over even their precious vessels to them as a loan, especially in circumstances where a severe conflict had been going on for years and the Egyptians feared that the Israelites were preparing to flee the country. Secondly, how could Moses, from a legal and moral standpoint, tolerate allowing his nation to take possession of goods lent by others in this manner? Therefore, in our view, this account is incorrect. The correct view is that these ornaments belonged to the Children of Israel themselves. The Qur'an elsewhere uses the words 'from their ornaments' (min huliyyim). This also indicates that these were the ornaments of the Israelites.
'So we threw them, and thus did the Samiri cast' (fa-qadhafnaha fa-kadhalika alqa as-samiri). 'Qadhf' means to throw away or cast off. The elders said: 'We carried the heavy burden of the people with reluctance, purely out of a desire to serve them. But when we were forced, we threw it off completely.' Those who appreciate the nuance of the Arabic language can see that the three words 'hummilna', 'awzar', and 'qadhf' all express the sense of heavy burden these elders felt because of this trust. When Moses rebuked them, they feared that since the idol was made from the jewelry that was in their custody, their Prophet might suspect that they too had a hand in this conspiracy. To clear any such suspicion, they clarified their position: 'We had no part in this fitnah. However, the burden of the people's jewelry that was placed on us was thrown off by us under the pressure of the people's demand, letting them do what they wished.' Their statement 'we did not do this of our own accord' also implies that they tried to save the people from the Samiri's fitnah to the best of their ability, but the situation had deteriorated so much that it was beyond their control. Since their helplessness was obvious, it appears that Moses accepted their excuse.
'And thus did the Samiri cast' (fa-kadhalika alqa as-samiri). The word 'alqa' in Arabic is used in a wide sense. Just as it means throwing or tossing something on the ground, it also means casting a lot, showing a skill, or performing a trick. Inshallah, we will discuss this in detail in Surah Al-Hajj. Here, the word is used in the sense of showing a skill or a trick. In verse 65 of this very Surah, it says: 'Whether you cast first, or we shall be the first to cast' (imma an tulqiya wa-imma an nakuna awwala man alqa) (The magicians said to Moses: 'Either you show your trick first, or we shall show ours').
The elders placed the entire responsibility of this fitnah on the Samiri, stating: 'We were forced to throw off the burden of the jewelry, and then the Samiri showed his trick and presented a calf.'

Thus, the Samiri brought forth a calf by molding. 'Jasadan lahu khuwar'. 'Khuwar' refers to the lowing sound of a calf. That is, he made the body of a calf from which a lowing sound emerged. It appears that the Samiri was an expert in the Egyptian art of sculpting. During that era, this art was at its peak in Egypt. In the commentary of Al-A'raf, we mentioned some rare artifacts of that period. It is not difficult for a skilled person to make a hollow body of a calf in such a way that when the wind passes through, it makes a sound like a calf's lowing. The Samiri claimed this sound was a result of his miracle and, to make fools of the masses, convinced them that he had put inside it the dust from the hoof of Gabriel's horse, which caused this sound. Those who put on such pseudo-spiritual acts can easily fool people even today, and much bigger Samiris than him exist in our cities today; so in that era, was it difficult for any Samiri to make fools of a simple-minded nation like the Children of Israel? Consequently, the troublemakers who initiated this fitnah began a propaganda campaign saying: 'In reality, this is your deity and the deity of Moses, but Moses has forgotten and gone off to Mount Tur in search of it.' The effect of this propaganda was that a vast majority of the Children of Israel fell into this trap.

This is an expression of astonishment at the simplicity of those simple-minded people—that these fools did not even consider that this lifeless body, which could neither answer their words nor bring them any harm or benefit, was of what use that they made it a deity! A deity is not a toy, but rather the greatest need of life. After all, what need of theirs was going to be fulfilled by it that they began worshipping it!

Exoneration of Aaron from the Fitnah
This is an expression of Aaron's innocence in this fitnah. As we mentioned above, the Torah attributes the responsibility for this entire trial to Aaron, but the Qur'an, in very clear terms, completely exonerates him from it. The phrase 'from before' (min qablu) also makes it clear that as soon as he saw the signs of this fitnah arising, he began warning the people: 'O people, these troublemakers are putting you in trial. Your Lord is the Most Merciful, not some calf molded by your own hands, so follow me and do not follow these corrupters.'

Aaron tried his best to warn the people, but no one listened to him. The people replied: 'Now we will remain devoted to its worship until Moses returns to us, and we will see what he does when he comes.' This phrase shows that although the people, falling for the Samiri's trick, committed a grave folly despite all of Aaron's efforts, later many of them, as is clear from Surah Al-A'raf, realized their folly. But once a folly is committed, it is not easy to rid oneself of it. Consequently, instead of immediately announcing their abandonment of it, these people felt compelled to persist in this ignorance until Moses's return.

Verses 92-93
Interrogation of Aaron
After questioning the elders of the nation, Moses turned towards Aaron, upon whom lay the primary responsibility of looking after the nation. He asked him: 'When you saw them going astray, why did you not follow my guidance? Did you disobey my command?' This command refers to the one he had given to Aaron and his appointed monitors when leaving for Mount Tur. We have also mentioned this under verse 142 of Surah Al-A'raf, where he had instructed Aaron: 'Take my place among my people after me, keep rectifying, and be vigilant against the mischievous elements in the nation, not letting them raise their heads.' The tone of Moses's question suggests that he did not assume Aaron had intentionally disobeyed his command, but rather he wanted to know his excuse: what constraint arose that prevented him from checking this fitnah and allowed it to raise its head?
After 'ma mana'aka' (what prevented you), there was apparently no need for 'la' in 'alla tattabi'ani', which is why our grammarians consider it redundant. However, it is not redundant; rather, it is used for emphasis in accordance with the established usage of the language. There are many examples of this in Arabic speech. This style also exists in our language.

The Excuse of Aaron
Moses caught Aaron by his head and his beard, shaking him to ask: 'What weakness have you shown!' This behavior of Moses highlights his zeal for the truth and also the love he naturally had for Aaron. The demand of this love was that when he felt a weakness had been committed by Aaron in the matter of religion, he was overcome with emotion and shook him severely. The way of the Prophets and the righteous is that they show leniency in worldly matters, but in religious matters they do not compromise. In this regard, the closer someone is to them, the more sensitive they are about them. The way of worldly people is entirely different. They are very alert and clever in their worldly matters, holding even their father and son to account, but in the matter of religion, they are completely indifferent and insensitive. Their brothers and relatives may do whatever they want, yet their honor is not stirred in the least!
Aaron presented his excuse in an extremely affectionate tone of address. He said: 'O son of my mother! Do not seize me by my beard or by my head. I feared that if I took a harsh step, division would break out among the Children of Israel, and you would then say: 'You did not act upon my advice and caused division within the nation.' In the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf, we explained that Aaron tried his utmost to stop the people from this fitnah, but the magic of the Samiri had worked so heavily on the vast majority that, far from stopping them, there was a fear that the wicked might kill Aaron. Now only two options remained: either Aaron should separate himself along with his companions, or wait a few days for Moses's return. In the first option, there was a fear of national division and mutual bloodshed. In the second option, there was a hope that Moses, with his awe and excellent handling, would control the situation. Based on this hope, he did not choose the first option, as it threatened mischief instead of reform, and he wished to keep the nation safe from this mischief as much as possible.

'[Moses] said, 'And what is your case, O Samiri?'
Interrogation of Samiri
After questioning all the responsible parties and when investigation established that the real instigator of this entire mischief was indeed the Samiri, Moses turned to him and asked him in a very sharp manner: 'O Samiri, tell me, what have you done? What is this matter? What is this fitnah you have raised?'

The Samiri's Pretext
When the Samiri saw that he was now in the grip of Moses, he very cleverly fabricated an excuse in which, on one hand, he tried to present a reason for his action, and at the same time admitted that it had now become clear to him that whatever he had understood was merely a deception of his own soul, and due to a misunderstanding, he had taken the deception of his ego to be a reality.
The story he made up was: 'I had a mystical vision that was granted only to me and not to others—namely, that I saw Gabriel arrive, and I took a handful of dust from his footprints (or the footprints of his horse) and threw it into the calf I made, which made it low. But now, after your warning, the reality has become clear to me that this was not a mystical vision, but merely a deception of my own soul, falling into which I committed this crime.' In this way, the Samiri tried to excuse himself before Moses, claiming that whatever he did was under the influence of a vision, and not with any malicious intent of his own. But he was well aware that escaping from Moses's grip would not be easy, and that this kind of pretext would not work before him; hence, he also made this admission that whatever happened was merely the result of a misconception, wherein he mistook a deception of his soul for a vision, leading to this crime.

The Curse upon the Samiri
With this excuse, his cunning and wickedness became fully clear to Moses. He cursed him and sentenced him to a lifelong punishment: that he would go about saying: 'O people, I am unclean (untouchable), let no one touch me, nor can I touch anyone.' Along with this, he warned him of the punishment in the Hereafter—that a promised day of punishment is coming for him from which he will not be able to save himself by any means. It is bound to happen.
'Go then!' (fa-dhhab) means get away, be expelled. It is used here in the sense of banishment.
'In this life' (fi al-hayati) means this punishment of curse is not temporary or transient, but rather it will be imposed upon you for your entire life.
The Meaning of 'La Misasa'
'That you say: No touching' (an taqula la misasa) means you will go about calling out with your own tongue: 'I am unclean; let no one touch me, and I am not permitted to touch anyone.'
In the Shariah of Moses, the punishment of cutting off a person from the congregation exists. In Leviticus Chapter 7 Verse 21, after stating the prohibition of certain things, this punishment is described for the perpetrator:
'the person who eats it shall be cut off from his people.'
The person who was given this punishment became a complete outcast. No one would participate in his joys or sorrows, he was not allowed to enter the place of worship, and no one would participate in his funeral rites.
An even harsher punishment is described for lepers. Observe the words of Leviticus:
'And the leper in whom the plague is, his clothes shall be rent, and his head bare, and he shall put a covering upon his upper lip, and shall cry, Unclean, unclean. All the days wherein the plague shall be in him he shall be defiled; he is unclean: he shall dwell alone; without the camp shall his habitation be.' (Leviticus 13:45-46)
My interpretation: Moses gave this very punishment to the Samiri; the only difference is that for the leper, this punishment was only as long as he was afflicted with the disease, but Moses gave this punishment to the Samiri for his entire life, because he was afflicted with the leprosy of hypocrisy, which is an ethical and spiritual leprosy far more vile than physical leprosy. Furthermore, Moses cursed him, which is the severest punishment in religion. When the Qur'an's words 'an taqula la misas' (that you say: no touching) and the Torah's words 'and cry, Unclean, unclean' are placed side by side, only then can the true position, context, and force of the Qur'an's words be understood. It must be kept in mind that for someone to cry out and declare their own uncleanness is the ultimate limit of humiliation upon humiliation!
The Fate of the Samiri's Deity
'And look at your god...' (Wa-anzur ila ilahika alladhi...). Along with this, Moses said regarding the deity he had molded: 'Behold, we will burn it before your eyes, then grind it to dust and scatter it into the sea.' Moses took this action because, along with shirk (idolatry), it is necessary to wipe out the remnants of shirk so that all its traces are eliminated at once. Our Prophet (peace be upon him) also completely eliminated all idols on the occasion of the Conquest of Makkah.
Here, a question may arise in some minds: why did Moses not execute the Samiri, when he had many perpetrators of this very crime killed, as mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah? In our view, the answer is that this curse, as we mentioned in relation to the people of the Sabbath, is a far more serious and exemplary punishment than death. The punishment of execution ends in an instant, and the lesson it offers to others is also temporary, but this punishment of a curse clings to a person in both this world and the Hereafter. Imagine the wretched fate of that human being who, throughout his life, proclaims with his own tongue: 'O people, I am unclean, let no one touch me!' It should also be kept in mind that a Prophet's curse is not something from which one can run and escape. The cursed one of a Prophet remains cursed wherever he goes, in this world and in the Hereafter!
A Curious Narrative of the Torah
Regarding this idol, the Qur'an's instruction is that Moses burned it, ground it, and scattered its ashes in the sea, but the Torah presents this curious narrative that Moses dissolved its ashes in water and made the people drink it. From this, one can estimate that the narrators of the Torah did not even possess the skill to fabricate a coherent narrative! What kind of refreshing drink was this that Moses made such arrangements to make his nation drink it? If this narrative were to be accepted as true, then it would also have to be accepted that perhaps it is the effect of this very drink that, despite being the bearers of the Book, polytheism is deeply ingrained in the nature of this unfortunate nation, from which they have never found deliverance and perhaps never will!

Meaning, those who made this calf a deity brought about their own doom. Your deity is not this calf, but only that Allah besides Whom there is no deity. 'His knowledge encompasses all things' (Wasi'a kulla shay'in 'ilman) is a proof of Tawhid (Monotheism), indicating that His knowledge encompasses everything. Nothing is outside the domain of His knowledge, so why should He associate anyone with Himself, and why should others declare anyone as His partner? When He alone hears and knows everything, He alone is sufficient. Where does any need for another remain?

The Subject Matter Ahead: Verses 99-135
The story of Moses has concluded. Now, the same subject with which the Surah began reappears with a new style and new arguments. Addressing the Holy Prophet, He says: 'These stories of the past that We have narrated to you are not merely historical tales; rather, this very same scenario is unfolding and will unfold before you and your opponents.' Those who turn away from this reminder and try to alienate others will carry their own burden on the Day of Resurrection, and they will also have to carry a portion of the burdens of those who were led astray because of them. That Day of Resurrection will be an extremely harsh day. On that day, no one will be able to assert themselves. Everyone will appear before God alone, and no one will be able to speak for intercession before Him without His permission. Thus, the reminder We have sent down to you warns people of every danger. Recite it to the people. Whoever is blessed with the ability will benefit from it; as for the one who turns away, leave him to his state. You bear no responsibility for him. Recite this Qur'an to the people in the gradual sequence with which it is being revealed; there is wisdom in this gradual sequence and order. Do not hasten with it. There is no good or blessing in haste. Adam hastened, and its result appeared to him in the form of Satan expelling him from Paradise. Direct those who demand a sign of punishment from you to the history of their own land, and bear their sarcasm and mockery with patience, and establish prayer with the utmost diligence to attain this patience. You do not need to be overly anxious about the faith of the wealthy and the elites. You are not in need of their wealth and assets, nor does your call depend on their patronage. Allah is the Protector and Sustainer of you and your companions. Tell those who demand a sign from you that the greatest sign has already come to you in the form of the Qur'an, and it has come so that the proof may be completed against you. If you do not consider this sufficient and are only seeking some punishment, then wait, and I too am waiting with you. Recite the upcoming verses in this light.
A Mirror of both the Past and the Future
The word 'kadhalika' (thus) refers to the story of Moses that has just passed. Now, addressing the Prophet, it says: 'We are narrating to you these important stories of the past and have bestowed upon you a Reminder directly from Us.' That is, what God did in the past with His messengers and their opponents has been narrated to you, and what He is going to do with these opponents of yours has also been told to you. Now, in this mirror, view your own future and that of your rivals. 'Dhikr' (Reminder) refers to the Holy Qur'an. In the Qur'an, Allah has preserved that portion of the teachings of previous prophets which was meant to be preserved, and it also contains the guidance that was to be revealed to the final Messenger.

Verses 100-101
Threat to the Opponents
This is a warning to those who turn away from this reminder. It states that those who turn away from it will carry a heavy burden on the Day of Resurrection. They will bear the burden of their own misguidance as well as a portion of the burdens of those whom they led astray. In 'khalidina fihi' (abiding eternally therein), the pronoun refers to the consequence of the word 'wizr' (burden); since 'wizr' refers to its inevitable outcome, namely the punishment, it is stated that they will remain in it forever. Regarding this burden, it is said that it will be an extremely evil burden, because they will never find deliverance from it, nor will anyone be a helper or supporter in carrying it. Everyone will have to carry this burden on their own.

Verse 102
'Zurq' is the plural of 'Azraq'. 'Azraq' refers to blue-eyed, but in Arabic idiom, it is also used for a frightened and terrified person, because in a state of intense fear, the eyes turn blue and their red veins disappear.
This is a depiction of the Day of Resurrection mentioned in the preceding verse—to remember the day when the Trumpet will be blown. The passive voice ('yunfakhu', will be blown) is used to express its dreadfulness, indicating that a terrifying sound will create a commotion, but no one will know where this sound is coming from. It states that on that day, We will gather all those criminals who turned away from Our reminder and are turning others away as well. Today they are boldly mocking Our warning, but on that day, everyone's eyes will be turned blue with fear.

Dispelling a Misconception
Meaning, today they are completely carefree and unafraid of the Resurrection. Firstly, they consider it highly improbable, and even if they accept it to some degree, they remain unconcerned, thinking that life, then death, then the barzakh (intermediary state), and then the Resurrection is such a distant matter that ruining one's present pleasures by worrying about it now is foolishness. It states that today they consider it a very distant thing, but when it stands before them, they will whisper to one another, saying: 'At most, only ten days have passed through all these stages!' We have explained elsewhere that on the Day of Resurrection, there will be no perception of the life in barzakh; it will feel as if one had just fallen asleep and just woken up. As for this worldly life, as long as it lasts, a person remains under the illusion that much of life is still left, but when the final hour arrives, one realizes that what was thought to be very long turned out to be extremely short. Especially, a life spent in negligence feels like nothing but a dream once the eyes are opened. The Qur'an warns against this very negligence here, emphasizing that the real gauge is not today's perception—which is a total illusion—but rather the perception that will occur after the reality is manifested.

That is, let no one think that the news We are giving of the conditions and perceptions of that day are mere empty words spoken only to intimidate. Rather, these are realities that will unfold. We are best aware of what they will say on that day. Leave aside this talk of ten days; on that day, the one among them deemed most sensible, wise, and accurate in estimation will say: 'Ten days? No, you tarried in the world for at most a single day!'
The Meaning of 'Amthal'
'Amthal' means the most excellent or superior. Here, this word is used by way of sarcasm, meaning that the one considered the most clever and knowledgeable among them will provide this proof of his estimation!
