Those who are familiar with the poetry of pre-Islamic Arabia know that many poets, falling into this very misconception warned against in these verses, declared the Resurrection to be 'hadith khurafah' (a mythical tale)—i.e., a useless talk. I am intentionally refraining from quoting those verses, and there is no particular need to quote them either, because this misconception is not specific to the era of ignorance alone, but is also the primary misconception of the people of this civilized age. ... The Qur'an has warned against this very misconception.

Verses 105-107
The Meaning of 'Nasf'
'Nasf' means to uproot something completely, to winnow grain in a winnowing basket, or to turn something into straw, dust, or ashes and blow it away in the wind.
In 'yadharuha' (He will leave it), the pronoun refers to the earth. In Arabic, pronouns are introduced in this manner for prominent and well-known things. In the Qur'an, pronouns are brought for the sky, the earth, etc., in several places. In 'He would not leave on its back any creature' (ma taraka 'ala zahriha min dabbatin), the pronoun also refers to the earth, even though it is not explicitly mentioned in the text.
'Qa'an safsafan' refers to a flat and barren plain, containing neither elevations nor depressions, nor vegetation, nor trees, nor forests.
'Amt' refers to elevation and height.
The Theory of the Eternity of Mountains and Its Refutation
Just as the Arabs held the aforementioned misconception regarding the Resurrection, they were also in the grip of the misconception of how it could be possible that one day all these mountains would disappear from the face of the earth. Let alone the commoners, even many of their intellectuals believed that mountains were immortal. Zuhayr, one of the wise poets of Arabia, says:
'Behold! I do not see anything remaining against the calamities of time,
Nor anything immortal except the firm mountains.'
When the Qur'an warned people of the earthquake of Resurrection, many of them, out of this misconception, would sarcastically and mockingly ask the Prophet: 'What will become of these grand mountains when the Resurrection comes? Will it uproot them too?' Say to them: 'Yes, my Lord will reduce them to dust and blow them away, and He will leave this earth as a completely barren, flat plain, with no sign of vegetation or trees, and no valleys, hills, or elevations and depressions!'
Pre-Islamic Arabs considered mountains to be invincible because, to them, mountains were the greatest things. However, the modern human considers himself invincible and is under the delusion that he will soon conquer the entire universe. In the presence of this delusion, it is obvious that the question of Resurrection is entirely out of the question for him!

'Hams' means a low and hushed sound, like a whisper.
It says: Today, people are not listening to Our caller. He calls them to the straight path, yet they walk crookedly and scream in opposition to him. But on that day, when the caller summons, everyone will follow him; no one will have the audacity to deviate even a hair's breadth, and before the awe of the Most Merciful, all voices will be hushed. Not a sound will come out of anyone's throat; whoever speaks will speak only in a whisper.

Verse 109
Conditions for Intercession
In my view, the exception (istithna) here is broken (munqati'). This is a refutation of the intercession presumed by the Arabs. Their belief was that the deities they worshipped were the darlings and favorites of God. They believed these deities would step forward on their own to intercede for whichever of their worshippers they pleased and save them from God. In refuting this belief, it states that on that day, no one's intercession will be of any benefit, except if the Most Merciful permits someone to intercede and is pleased with what they say on behalf of another. That is, the one who intercedes will do so only with God's permission, and the one on whose behalf intercession is made is also conditioned upon God approving of someone speaking for them. The point is that effective intercession is simultaneously bound by these two conditions. If someone has formulated a theory regarding intercession by disregarding these conditions, it is merely their delusion, the reality of which will confront them on the Day of Resurrection. Inshallah, further explanation of this subject will appear under verse 28 of Surah Al-Anbiya.

The commentary of this phrase has been explained in detail under Ayat al-Kursi. The meaning is that Allah's knowledge encompasses all. He is aware of everyone's past and future, and their present and absent. No one else has the capacity for their knowledge to encompass God's knowledge; so who is in a position to add anything to God's knowledge regarding anyone and, on that basis, make an intercession in their favor! God is not in need of learning from anyone what kind of person someone is, what their deeds have been, and what treatment they deserve. All these things are present in His knowledge. Please also take a look at the discussion that has passed on this issue under verse 87 of Surah Maryam.

'Anu' means showing humility and self-abasement, and 'zulm' refers to shirk (polytheism), as we have clarified in several places.
Refutation of a Notion Held by those Wishing for False Intercession
This is a refutation of another false notion of those who hope for false intercession. Regarding their self-proclaimed partners whom they deemed intercessors, they assumed that these partners held a position of great coddling and intimate influence before God. They believed these partners could present whatever they wished before God with audacity, confidence, and persistence, and that God so cherished pampering them that He would inevitably accept their word and grant their intercession. It says: let those who are in the grip of such an illusion remove it from their minds. No one holds a position of coddling or intimate insistence before God. Instead of intimacy and insistence, all faces will be humbled before Him in awe and submission.
Here, reference is made to 'Al-Hayy' (the Living) and 'Al-Qayyum' (the Sustainer) from the Beautiful Names, and the citation of these names has a specific relevance. Regarding the Arab polytheists, we have clarified that as far as believing in God is concerned, they did believe in Him, but under their polytheistic beliefs, they had reduced His status—Allah forbid—to that of an old, inactive family elder who hands over all responsibilities to others and sits retired in a corner. This concept made them completely indifferent to God and anchored all their trust in their self-proclaimed partners and intercessors. Here, the Qur'an strikes a blow to this false notion and declares that God is not an inactive entity, but rather a living God, and He has not left all the affairs of this world He created to others to sit silently in a corner as a distant first cause; rather, He is Qayyum, meaning He maintains the entire order of this universe and runs it under His own direct supervision and management.
Shirk Leads to Failure
'And indeed, he who carries injustice has failed' (Wa-qad khaba man hamala zulman). 'Zulm' here means shirk, and the indefinite form of this word here is a proof that whoever commits any type of shirk will be a loser and failed in the Hereafter. It must be kept in mind here that shirk is not limited to practicing idol-worship; rather, it has many manifest and hidden types, and every type of it causes loss and failure. We are explaining its various types in this book from time to time, and we have also written a dedicated book on this subject titled 'The Reality of Shirk'. Those who seek details may read it.

The Meaning of 'Zulm' and 'Hadm'
When the words 'zulm' and 'hadm' are used together, 'zulm' contains the sense of deprivation of rights, while 'hadm' contains the sense of usurpation and transgression. That is, one scenario is that you withhold someone's right that is due upon you—this is 'zulm'. The second scenario is that you transgress and attempt to seize and usurp what belongs to another—this is 'hadm'.
In the preceding verse, it was said that whoever commits any type of shirk will fail. In contrast to that, it is stated here that whoever performs righteous deeds and is also a believer will receive a full reward for their actions. They will fear neither any deprivation of rights nor any transgression.
The meaning of 'while he is a believer' (wa huwa mu'min) is obviously that he performs good deeds while remaining firm on Tawhid (Monotheism). If there is any pollution of shirk in it, then his actions have no value in the sight of God. If an action is not performed for the pleasure of God but for some other purpose, it has no consideration before God, even if it is apparently a good deed!

The Original Line of Discourse with a New Introduction
This is linked to the discussion above, from where this sequence of discourse started. In verses 99-100, it was stated: 'We have given you from Us a reminder (the Qur'an), and whoever turns away from it will bear a heavy burden on the Day of Resurrection.' Following that, the mention of the Resurrection appeared for warning. After the mention of the Resurrection, the same original subject reappears with a new introduction and further details. It says: 'For this very purpose of admonition, We have sent down this Qur'an in the Arabic language, and in it We have clarified in various ways the consequences and results that its deniers must inevitably face, so that if people wish to adopt the path of piety and fear of God, they may do so, and if not, then at least it serves as a reminder from Us, so that if they are seized by Us, they cannot make the excuse that they were not warned.'
Completion of Proof by the Qur'an upon the Arabs
The words 'an Arabic Qur'an' (Qur'anan 'Arabiyyan) are used here as an expression of favor and grace upon the Arabs, and also for completing the proof and cutting off all excuses. The meaning is that by sending down this Qur'an in the Arabic language, He bestowed His greatest favor upon them by selecting their own language for His admonition and teaching, and in this way, the proof was completed against them. Now, on the Day of Resurrection, they cannot make the excuse that if a Messenger had come to them with a Book in their own language, they would have been the most guided of people. This Qur'an has eliminated all such excuses!
'And We have diversified in it the warnings' (wa sarrafna fihi mina al-wa'idi). We have explained in several places that 'tasrif' means presenting something through various and diverse methods. The meaning is that, first of all, We sent down this Book in their own Arabic language, and then We did not leave the consequences of turning away from it ambiguous, but rather demonstrated them thoroughly through arguments of reason, narration, history, the horizons, and their own souls. Now, if even after this arrangement they do not benefit from it, the responsibility for the consequences lies entirely upon them!
'That perhaps they will fear, or it would cause them to remember' (la'allahum yattaquna aw yuhdithu lahum dhikra) means the primary objective of this entire arrangement is to produce piety and fear of God within them, and if not, then it should serve as a reminder to them of why they were brought into this world and what they are currently making of it. It should be kept in mind that the Qur'an has also reminded the Arabs of their original history—when Abraham settled their father Ishmael in this land with a specific mission. This has been explained in detail in other places. The word 'yuhdithu' (brings about/causes) in the verse points to this aspect. The primary meaning of 'ihdath-i dhikr' is the renewed reminder of a past history.

The True Reason for the Prophet's Restlessness for the Revelation of the Qur'an
To understand the correct meaning of this verse, please look at what we wrote under verses 64-65 of Surah Maryam. To counter all the opposition and mockery of the opponents, the Holy Prophet had only one weapon: the Qur'an. For this reason, there was naturally a restlessness and anxiety in him for its revelation. From it, he received guidance in difficulties; this very thing served as a source of comfort in confusions and worries; and the decisive answers to the new demands and objections raised daily by the opponents could also be provided only by the Qur'an. For these reasons, like a soldier fighting against a vast army of enemies, he was always waiting for this divine reinforcement. To relieve this anxiety of the Prophet, it was said: 'Do not hasten with the Qur'an before its revelation is completed to you.' God's essence is most high and exalted. He alone knows His wisdom and benefits. He is the true King of this universe, and the reminder you are reciting to the people is the command of this very true King. This is not the petition of a seeker. Remain completely indifferent to the people's rejection or acceptance and keep reciting it to them in the gradual order and sequence in which it is being revealed. God, Who has fixed a term for its completion, is well aware of its wisdom and benefit. Do not hasten for it to be sent down before that term. However, keep making this prayer: 'O my Lord, increase me in knowledge!'
This subject is mentioned in various places in the Qur'an, and in each place, it is presented in the exact same context. Our commentators have taken these verses in a narrow sense, suggesting that when Gabriel recited the revelation to the Prophet, he would hasten to memorize it, for which he was corrected. Although it is true in itself that for whatever one is passionate and restless, the emotion of longing makes one hasty—if we receive a love letter for which we have spent anxious hours, we would want to read it all in a single breath; this emotion is natural, and regarding the Qur'an, this emotion must have been present in the Prophet—but it is not correct that he was corrected for this here. Its real background is the one we have indicated. Verses of this subject will appear in other places as well, through which, Inshallah, the matter will become fully clear.

We have explained the meaning of 'ahida ila' (made a covenant with) elsewhere. This style of expression is used to give a specific instruction or testament with emphasis and care.
Instruction to Avoid Haste
In the preceding verse, the Prophet was prohibited from hastening in the matter of the Qur'an. Now, to clarify the harms of this haste, reference is made to the story of Adam and Iblis (Satan), showing that this very haste became a cause of stumbling for Adam. Taking advantage of this very weakness of human nature, Iblis deceived Adam and made him neglectful of the instruction Allah had given him to avoid the fruit of a specific tree. The ultimate result of this was that Adam had to leave Paradise and come to the testing ground of this world. The purpose of reminding this story is that this haste has never suited anyone. Above, in the story of Moses, it was also made clear that his haste did not suit him either; rather, it became a severe trial for him and his nation. Behind the veil of these events, the Prophet is taught not to hasten in the matter of his mission, but to persist in his work with patience and determination, step by step, as he is guided. If you hasten—even if for the best of purposes—there is a danger that the training of your Ummah might remain deficient, and some Satan or Samiri might take advantage of it and plunge the people into a fitnah.
'Before' (min qablu) is a reminder of past history. That is, just as you are being instructed today not to hasten, similarly, previously instructions had also been given to Adam, the father of you and the entire human race, but he could not maintain it, as a result of which he fell into the deception of Satan. Since past experiences, especially when they concern one's own ancestor, are highly educational for present and future generations, the gesture is made to keep past events and experiences in view.
'But he forgot, and We did not find in him determination' (fa-nasiya wa-lam najid lahu 'azman). Here, 'nasiya' is used in the sense of fading from sight. That is, the instruction given to Adam faded from his sight due to being overcome by a momentary urge of haste, and he could not remain firm on it with strong determination. The details of the instruction given to Adam and the lapse committed by him are coming ahead. In this verse, only this reality is clarified: all emotions and urges are parts of human nature, but there are limits and boundaries for them. If they remain within their limits, they are a source of goodness and blessing for both the individual and society; but if the reins of any of them are loosened slightly, it can become a danger for both the individual and society. To keep these urges and emotions under control, a person has to develop the qualities of patience and determination within themselves. By developing this quality, a person can control their desires and self; otherwise, who knows which emotion might plunge them into which trial. Haste is also one of the human emotions, and it is built into human nature. In verses like 'Man was created of haste' (Al-Anbiya: 37) and 'Man is ever hasty' (Al-Isra: 11), reference is made to this reality, but this is beneficial for man only as long as it remains under the control of his patience and determination. If it overcomes patience and determination, it can become a very great danger for him.
'And We did not find in him determination' (wa-lam najid lahu 'azman). The correct meaning of 'azm' is patience, resolve, and steadfastness, the opposite of which is impatience and hastiness. Here, the primary purpose of the discourse is to point toward Adam's impatience and hastiness and to warn the Prophet of its consequences.

The preceding verse presented the summary of the story of Adam and Iblis that is intended here. Following this, the details of the actual story are now presented. Placing a summary before the details of stories greatly helps in understanding the coherence of the text, and the Qur'an has adopted this style of expression in various places. The Qur'an also adopted this very style in narrating the event of the Companions of the Cave, and we have explained this in its context.
The Lesson from the Story of Adam and Iblis
It says: Remember when We commanded the angels to show respect to Adam, and they all complied with this command except Iblis, who refused to comply. This event has also been mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah and Al-A'raf, and we have discussed every part of it in detail there. Repeating it here would cause unnecessary length. Please refer to what has been written under verses 30-39 of Al-Baqarah in Tadabbur-i-Qur'an, Volume I, pages 112-130, and under verses 11-14 of Al-A'raf. We have discussed the wisdom behind this command, the nature of the prostration, the reason for the inclusion of the jinn with the angels in this command, and Iblis and his pride and arrogance, to the extent of our knowledge.

Iblis did not merely refuse to prostrate; rather, as we have explained in detail under verses 14-22 of Surah Al-A'raf, in his fit of jealousy, he challenged Allah Himself, saying that if He granted him respite to mislead Adam and his offspring, he would prove that they are neither worthy of any superiority over him nor deserving of this Paradise; instead, he would make most of them his followers and allies. Allah, on one hand, granted him respite, telling him to go and mislead whoever he could, warning that He would fling him and all his followers into Hell; and on the other hand, He warned Adam: 'Live in Paradise, but keep in mind that Iblis is your eternal enemy. Let it not happen that he misleads you and causes you to be expelled from Paradise, leaving you deprived and unsuccessful.'

Verses 118-119
A Comprehensive Definition of Adam's Paradise
In these two verses, there is an extremely comprehensive description of the Paradise in which Adam was placed. It states that in it, you will neither go hungry nor naked, nor will you suffer from thirst or the heat of the sun. In other words, to express this, one could say: in it, you will remain safe from the affliction of both cold and heat. Among the afflictions of cold are hunger and nakedness, and among the discomforts of heat are thirst and the sun. Those who are familiar with the speech of the Arabs know that they sometimes refer to the discomforts of cold and heat in exactly these terms. Some people thought that mentioning thirst along with hunger, and the sun along with nakedness, would have been more appropriate. In our view, this opinion is the result of a lack of appreciation for the nuances of the Arabic language.

Verse 120
Satan's Successful Attack on Adam
Although Iblis had been expelled from Paradise due to his arrogance and disobedience of God's command, he had obtained respite from Allah to test his schemes on Adam and the children of Adam until the Day of Resurrection. For this reason, he approached Adam even in Paradise to whisper doubts to him, explaining: 'The secret behind you being barred from this specific tree is that it is the tree of eternal life and eternal kingdom. If you desire eternal life and an imperishable kingdom, taste the fruit of this tree; otherwise, what guarantee is there that you will remain in this Paradise forever!' Firstly, humans possess a weakness of becoming greedy for whatever they are restricted from. Secondly, Adam also had a lingering fear that he might be expelled from this Paradise, since Allah had warned him: 'Iblis is your enemy, lest he cause you to be expelled from Paradise.' Iblis sensed this very apprehension of Adam and, in a highly well-wishing manner, succeeded in making him believe that if he desired eternal life and an imperishable kingdom, he must eat the fruit of this tree. The dominance of this desire made him completely neglectful of the warning Allah had given him regarding Satan's enmity. He mistook his enemy for a well-wisher and fell for his deception.
From this, it is known that although Satan is the eternal enemy of the children of Adam, his most successful attack is the one he launches in the guise of a friend and well-wisher. He comes in this very guise to whisper doubts, making man believe that the secret to all his civilization and progress lies in choosing the very things that God and His Messenger have prohibited him from. Analyze today's circumstances, and you will find that the greatest argument presented by Satan's agents for making all the prohibitions of Shariah permissible is that if religion's restrictions are followed in these matters, it will sound the death knell of all civilization and progress!
Dispelling a Doubt
The subject matter mentioned in this verse appears in Surah Al-A'raf as: 'Your Lord did not forbid you this tree except lest you become angels or become of the immortal' (20). Here, the question arises that in the verse under discussion, the words 'a kingdom that does not decay' (mulkin la yabla) are used, whereas in Al-A'raf, the words are 'lest you become angels' (an takuna malakayni). Although there is no contradiction between these two, a difference certainly exists; so what is the way to reconcile them? To escape this difficulty, some people have read 'malakayni' as 'malikayni' with a kasrah on the lam, meaning: 'lest you two become kings', and thus tried to reconcile these two verses. But firstly, even in this case, complete reconciliation is not achieved, and secondly, in our view, the only valid recitation is the mutawatir and established recitation of the Mus'haf, and we do not consider the interpretation of any verse based on non-mutawatir recitations to be correct.
In our view, the answer to this question is that Iblis presented various types of temptations to Adam and Eve to bring them over to his side: sometimes he gave them the hope of becoming angels, and sometimes he gave them the expectation of attaining an imperishable kingdom. Our mind turns to this because in Al-A'raf, it is explicitly stated that Iblis had to put in a great deal of effort to trap Adam in his net. He succeeded in winning them over only after taking many oaths and giving great assurances. In verse 22 of Al-A'raf, the words 'So he misled them with deception' (fa-dallahuma bi-ghururin) are used. We wrote there while explaining them that their correct meaning is to bring someone over to one's side step-by-step or to smooth-talk them. This shows that Adam did not fall for Iblis's trick in a single sentence; rather, Iblis created various opportunities and gave them different kinds of deceptions until they fell into his trap. Sometimes he said: 'God has forbidden you this tree out of fear that you might become angels,' and sometimes he said: 'He has forbidden it so that you do not obtain an imperishable kingdom.' The objective was to trap them in his web of deception by some means or another, which is why he tried to make up whatever excuse worked at any given moment.

Verse 121
The explanation of this verse has already appeared in the commentary of Al-Baqarah and also under verse 22 of Al-A'raf; there is no room to repeat all those points here. 'Ghawa' means he went astray and failed (dalla wa khaba). The meaning of the verse is that Adam, falling for Iblis's trick, ate the fruit of the forbidden tree, as a result of which the garment of Paradise that had been put on them—and regarding which, as passed in verse 118, Allah's promise was that they would never be deprived of it—was stripped from them, and in panic, they began covering their bodies with the leaves of the garden. 'And Adam disobeyed his Lord and went astray' (Wa-'asa Adamu Rabbahu fa-ghawa), meaning they could not remain firm on the instruction Allah had given them, as a result of which they strayed from the correct path. 'And they began to fasten together over themselves from the leaves of Paradise' (wa-tafiqa yakhsifani 'alayhima min waraqi al-jannati) expresses the panic and dismay that overtook Adam and Eve upon suddenly being deprived of the garments of Paradise. They had taken this step in greed of obtaining an eternal Paradise, but the result was that even what they possessed was lost.

Verse 122
The Meaning of 'Ijtiba' and the Effect of Adam's Repentance
We have observed that the mention of 'ijtiba' (selection) here occurs before his repentance and the acceptance of his repentance. From this, it is known that this 'ijtiba' refers to granting Adam the ability to repent and bestowing upon him the words of repentance. In Surah Al-Baqarah, this same subject is mentioned as follows: 'Then Adam received from his Lord words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, He is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful' (37). In Surah Al-A'raf, the details of these words of repentance are also provided: 'They said, "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers"' (23). This shows that Adam was deeply remorseful of the lapse he committed, and it is Allah's rule that when a servant is ashamed of his sin, He grants him the ability to turn back and reform, and after repentance and reform, He draws him even closer to Himself than before.
This very thing has been termed 'ijtiba' here, and there is no doubt that extremely fortunate is that human being who, along with the feeling of remorse and the ability to repent, is also taught the words of repentance by the Generous Lord.
'Then He accepted his repentance and guided him' (taba 'alayhi wa-hada). When 'taba' is followed by 'ala', as we explained under Baqarah 37, it also carries the sense of mercy. That is, Allah accepted his repentance, showed mercy to him, and guided him. 'Guided him' obviously means giving guidance for the upcoming stages so that Adam and his offspring could counter the trials of Satan.
Refutation of Christians and Those Denying Adam's Prophethood
This verse also refutes the Christian belief in the eternal sinfulness of Adam and his offspring, and also refutes the view of those who consider Adam to be merely the father of the human race and do not accept him as a Prophet. The words of this verse are a proof of his prophethood, and intellect also demands that Allah bestow upon him and his offspring a Shariah suited to their circumstances for their guidance. The explanation of this is coming in the next verse.

Verse 123
All the aspects related to this verse have been discussed under verse 36 of Al-Baqarah and verse 24 of Al-A'raf. Under those verses, we also clarified the wisdom that necessitated Adam and his offspring to come into this world and counter Satan, and we also clarified that the address in these verses is not to Adam and Eve, but to Adam and Iblis as two opposing parties.
'Some of you as enemies to others' (Ba'dukum li-ba'din 'aduwwun), meaning Satan will be your enemy, and you—if your ultimate end is dear to you and you wish to regain Paradise—will be enemies to Satan. This is because every intelligent person considers his enemy as an enemy and remains vigilant against him; it is only the fool and the short-sighted who strike up a friendship with their enemy and act upon his advice.
Those who, based on the misconception that the dual form is used here, have assumed that the address is to Adam and Eve, are completely mistaken. Firstly, this exact same thing has been stated in Al-Baqarah and Al-A'raf etc. using the plural form. Secondly, if the dual form has been used here, it is accompanied by the word 'jami'an' (all together); if the address were only to Adam and Eve, this word would become entirely redundant. Thirdly, the natural relationship between Adam and Eve is not of enmity, but of love. If enmity occurs between husband and wife, it is not because they are naturally 'enemies to one another', but rather it arises from Satan's interference and mischief. However, the enmity between Satan and them is natural, and this enmity is until the Day of Resurrection. Satan's enmity is obvious, as it is based on jealousy, and he has openly declared it. As for man, if he possesses sense, he must always keep this reality in mind: he has been sent to this world after being expelled from Paradise specifically to battle this enemy. If he makes friendship with him, it means he is surrendering before his own enemy.
The Weapon to Counter Satan
'And if there should come to you guidance from Me, then whoever follows My guidance will neither go astray nor suffer' (Fa-imma ya'tiyannakum minni hudan fa-mani ittaba'a hudaya fala yadillu wa-la yashqa). This is the weapon that Allah promised to provide to Adam and the children of Adam to counter Satan. Since man has been sent to this world to battle a highly cunning enemy, Allah preferred to bless him with specific guidance from Himself alongside the guidance of intellect and nature, so that he could counter Satan with double strength and resolve. He said: 'Go down from here, you will be enemies to each other. Then, when guidance comes to you from Me, whoever follows My guidance will neither be misled by Satan's deception nor be deprived of that Paradise from which Satan has sworn to keep you deprived.'
Two things are proved quite clearly from this verse:
First, the only way to remain safe from the trials of Satan in this world is the Book of Allah; whoever is deprived of this light is at all times vulnerable to the devils of jinn and mankind.
Adam Was a Prophet
Second, Adam was a Prophet. When he was sent down to this world to battle Satan, and Allah's guidance was necessary for this battle, then inevitably, Allah must have granted him some Shariah suited to their circumstances for his guidance and that of his offspring. We have indicated some laws of Adam's Shariah in the commentary of Surah Al-Ma'idah under the story of Abel and Cain.

Verse 124
No Tranquility Without Faith
'Ma'ishatan dankan' means a depressed life, which is deprived of the blessing of peace, tranquility, contentment, and expansion of the breast (sharah-i sadr). It states that whoever turns away from Our reminder will live a life in this world deprived of peace of heart and expansion of the breast, and on the Day of Resurrection, We will gather him blind.
Tranquility of Heart is in the Remembrance of Allah
The reason why his life is deprived of peace of heart and expansion of the breast is that there is a void within man that cannot be filled by anything except faith in Allah. For this reason, until he obtains faith, no other thing can bless him with comfort and tranquility. Other things, no matter how magnificent and fascinating they may appear, can serve as temporary distractions, but cannot remove the restlessness of the heart and soul. When a baby cries out of hunger, it can be pacified for a while by giving it a pacifier or a nipple, but it finds real peace only when the mother presses it to her breast and feeds it milk. Without this, its restlessness does not go away. Such is the case of man: no matter what means and resources he gathers for himself, if he is deprived of faith in God, he will remain uncontented, wavering, apprehensive, anxious, and in a state of inner turmoil, even though he may try to conceal it with his outward displays. The sovereignty of the contented soul (nafs mutma'innah) is obtained only through true and firm faith. 'Verily, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest!'
A Question and Its Answer
It is possible that a question may arise in someone's mind: many people claim to have faith, yet their lives are highly troubled and disorganized. On the contrary, how many are there who consider God to be merely an illusion, yet they live a highly carefree and tranquil life? The answer to this is that, firstly, the discussion here is not regarding the claimants of faith, but the true believers. Secondly, those whom we consider tranquil despite being indifferent to God—how many dangers and anxieties are hidden within them, though they are not visible to everyone. Either they themselves see them, or those who possess spiritual insight can see them.
Those Neglectful of Allah's Remembrance Will Be Gathered Blind on the Day of Resurrection
'And We will gather him on the Day of Resurrection blind' (wa-nahshuruhu yawma al-qiyamati a'ma). Those who turn away from Allah's remembrance will be gathered blind on the Day of Resurrection. The reason for this is that the thing which lifts the veils from the eyes, ears, and hearts is Allah's reminder. If a person turns away from this reminder, then as stated in the Qur'an, despite possessing eyes, ears, and intellect, he is blind, deaf, and devoid of understanding. As a punishment for showing ingratitude for these great favors, Allah will gather such foolish animals as blind. Allah did not bestow these great blessings merely for slavery to sensory perceptions, but rather bestowed them for observing those realities that are inscribed on every atom and leaf of this universe. If a person sees everything but fails to see the very reality that is most prominent, then though he sees everything, he perceives nothing at all.
'I see everything, yet I perceive nothing at all!'
Answer to a Question
Here, a question arises: in Surah Qaf, it is said: 'So We have removed from you your cover, and your sight this Day is sharp' (22), and here it is said that We will gather him blind—how then to reconcile these two? The answer is that there is no contradiction between these two statements. What is stated in Surah Qaf is: 'You were completely heedless of this Resurrection, but today all realities have come before you and all veils have been torn away!' The particularly eloquent aspect within this style of speech is that in this world, the slaves of sensory perceptions live their lives as blind, but not only do they have no perception of their blindness, they actually consider themselves highly wise and insightful. But on the Day of Resurrection, their blindness will become apparent to them. Today, the case is such that the eyes of our scientists, despite being armed with all telescopes and microscopes, are incapable of even seeing their own eyes. But a day is coming when all veils will be removed, and no room for denial will remain for anyone; this uncovering of reality has been termed: 'so your sight this Day is sharp'.

Verses 125-126
'Nasiya' here means to ignore. The explanation of this meaning of the word has been given in another place.
It states that those who are gathered blind will cry out: 'O my Lord, we were people of sight, so why have You gathered us blind!' It will be said: 'We sent Our Book to you, whose verses clarified every single reality to you, but you ignored them and remained completely blind and deaf.' The point is: if you had eyes, why did those eyes not open despite all Our admonition and warning? When you did not open your eyes at that time, you deserve that your eyes remain closed now.
'And thus will you this Day be forgotten' (Wa-kadhalika al-yawma tunsa), meaning now this wailing and lamentation of yours is useless. Just as you ignored Our verses, similarly you deserve to be ignored today, and no attention will be paid to this cry of yours!

'Israf' means transgressing limits. Here, 'whoever transgresses' (man asrafa) refers to those who wasted their powers and capabilities on things they were meant to avoid, and shut their eyes to things they were meant to adopt. It states that these conditions of the Hereafter that have been described are how We will recompense those who waste their capabilities on a completely wrong target and do not believe in the verses of their Lord. Then it warns further that the punishment of the Hereafter is no ordinary thing. Let no one consider it light by comparing it to the warnings of this world. It will be extremely severe and most lasting.

The correct meaning of 'Afalam yahdi lahum' in our language would be: 'Has such and such thing not served as a guide for them and opened their eyes?'
Response to the Demand for a Sign of Punishment
This is the response to the demand for a sign of punishment from those who turn away from the Book of Allah. It states: they are warned of punishment, and they demand a sign of punishment; so has it not served as a guide for them how many generations We destroyed before them as a punishment for turning away from Our reminder and warning—and those nations did not belong to some other territory but were dwellers of this very land, over whose ruined remnants and ruins these people constantly pass during their trade journeys! This is an allusion to the dwellings of 'Ad and Thamud etc., whose mention is also found in the poetry of the Arabs and whose location along the public trade routes is well known. It states that if they want a sign, there are many signs present in the history of their own country and its ruins. Why, then, do they not take heed from them? A wise person is one who learns from the fate of others, rather than accepting it only when that same fate confronts him. If a human adopts this latter approach, then what remains the difference between him and an animal!

'Ajalun musamman' (a term decreed) is conjured to 'kalimatun' (a word).
This is the response to the demand for a sign of punishment. It states that if a word had not already preceded from your Lord, and a term had not been decreed, then the punishment for which they are making haste would have struck them today. That is, if there is a delay in its appearance, it is occurring under the sunnah (practice) of God and His established rules and regulations; let no one fall into the misconception that this is merely an empty threat, or that Allah is currently unable to arrange it, which is why the punishment is not coming. This subject appears in various places in the Qur'an: 'And if it were not for a word that preceded from your Lord for a specified term, it would have been judged between them' (Al-Shura: 14). This shows that for the judgment of the deniers and rejectors, Allah has fixed a Day of Account, whose period and day are also decreed; because of this, despite the haste of the hasty ones, He is granting them respite. Unfortunate are those who, instead of benefiting from this respite, make it a means to increase their transgression!
Here, 'kalimatun' refers to the decisive word (Kalimat al-Fasl)—meaning Allah has decreed from before that a day of judgment and justice will come, and 'ajalun musamman' points to the decreeing of that day, so that no one considers it vague or unknown; rather, it is recorded in God's calendar with the precision of minutes and seconds.

Injunction to the Prophet to Bear Patience and Emphasis on Prayer
Now, the Messenger is instructed to bear patience against all the absurd talk of the opponents, and to show the utmost diligence in prayer to attain this patience. We have shed light on the mutual relationship between patience and prayer in more than one place. The foundation of patience, in reality, rests upon the belief that whatever occurs happens by the will of God, and His will is based entirely on goodness. To make this belief firm in the heart and present in the mind, the greatest and most effective means is prayer.
'And glorify with the praise of your Lord' (Wa-sabbih bi-hamdi Rabbika). The general meaning of 'tasbih' is the remembrance of Allah, and this remembrance is desired at all times; but here, it is mentioned with the restriction of specific times. Therefore, the context is a proof that it refers to prayer. The restriction of praise (hamd) along with glorification (tasbih) is to indicate the correct form of Allah's remembrance, which should contain both aspects: glorification and praise. We have explained elsewhere that 'tasbih' is dominated by the aspect of negation of flaws (tanzih), while 'hamd' is dominated by the aspect of affirmation of perfection. The correct form of remembering God is to declare Him free of all unfitting attributes and attribute to Him all high qualities. If even one of these aspects is not properly maintained, a person's concept regarding God becomes incorrect, shaking his entire system of thought. When some religions gave importance to tanzih, they exceeded the limits to such an extent that they turned God into a completely abstract entity, entirely disconnected from creation, as a result of which they had to seek the support of intermediaries and means. The Qur'an directed both glorification and praise to keep people safe from this fitnah, so that the servant's relationship with his Lord could be established on a correct foundation. Without establishing this relationship, a person's foot cannot stand firm on the straight path.
Teaching the Times of Prayer
'Before the rising of the sun and before its setting' (qabla tulu'i ash-shamsi wa-qabla ghurubiha) refers to the Fajr and 'Asr prayers. In Bukhari, this same narration is reported from the Prophet. The reason for mentioning these two prayers first is their importance in religion. The importance of these two prayers is also clear from other places in the Qur'an.
'And of the hours of the night glorify Him' (Wa-min ana'i al-layli fa-sabbih). And perform prayer during the hours of the night. In the hours of the night, there are two prayers: 'Isha and Tahajjud. Here, the repetition of the verb is a proof of emphasis. This emphasis is given because both of these prayers are highly heavy upon the self.
'And at the ends of the day' (wa-atrafa an-nahari). In the ends and parts of the day, there are three prayers: Chasht (Duha), Zuhr, and Maghrib. The placement of Chasht and Maghrib at the ends of the day is obvious. Reflect, and you will find that Zuhr is also at the ends of the day, because the day is divided into two parts; the time of Zuhr is at the final edge of its first half and the initial edge of its second half.
This verse quite clearly refutes the opinion of those who claim that the times of prayer are not mentioned in the Qur'an. In the Qur'an, not only the obligatory prayers but also the times of Ishraq and Tahajjud are mentioned. The Prophet, through his action, regulated these times and defined their boundaries. He also defined the forms of various prayers and clarified which of these prayers hold the status of obligatory (Fard/Wajib) and which hold the status of voluntary (Nawafil). Obviously, explaining all these details and defining the boundaries and limits of every prayer was the task of the Prophet, because he was not merely the reciter of the Qur'an but also its teacher.
Arranging Voluntary Prayers to Attain Patience and Determination
Keep in mind here that the emphasis on prayer in the verse under discussion is given as a measure to attain patience and determination. These verses were revealed in a highly difficult phase of the conflict between truth and falsehood. In them, the Prophet and, through him, the Ummah, are taught that measure which keeps one steadfast in difficulties and trials and makes one deserving of God's mercy and help. In such circumstances, not only the arrangement of obligatory prayers is required, but also the arrangement of voluntary prayers. The Qur'an, the statements of the Prophet, and the practice of the righteous predecessors—everything supports this view. In general circumstances, the status of Tahajjud and Ishraq is indeed that of voluntary prayers, but in difficulties and trials, whether individual or collective, their arrangement is necessary.
'Sing the camel-driver's song faster when you see the litter is heavy!'
Inshallah, we will discuss this issue in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Muzzammil and shed light on those benefits that are kept in view in defining the times of prayer.
Prayer Encompasses Everything
'That you may be pleased' (la'allaka tarda). In another place, this same subject is expressed as: 'And your Lord will surely give you, and you will be pleased' (Ad-Duha: 5). Within these words, a world of glad tidings is hidden. The meaning is: remain firm on your stance and keep busy with your work, and maintain the prayers; God will grant you such successes in both this world and the Hereafter that you will be delighted! Within these prayers, everything is preserved for you. You do not need to look toward anyone else besides your Lord.

Instruction of Indifference toward the Elites and the Wealthy
Now, the Prophet is stopped from being overly concerned with and accommodating of the elites and the wealthy of his nation. Since the elites and the wealthy of any nation hold great significance in the reform or corruption of that nation—their corruption leads to the corruption of the entire nation, and their reform offers hope for the reform of the entire nation—it has always been the practice of the Prophets to address the leaders and elders of their nations first, and to remain busy with reforming their thoughts until they lost hope due to their stubbornness and obstacy. In accordance with this practice of the Prophets, our Holy Prophet also first invited the chiefs and elites of Quraysh, hoping that if they accepted this truth, the call of truth and the poor Muslims would receive strength from their influence, power, and wealth. This desire was based entirely on well-wishing for these elites and the desire to elevate the word of Allah. But when these elites proved by their behavior that they were not going to soften, Allah stopped His Prophet from being overly anxious about them, saying: 'Do not pay any attention to their wealth and assets; your mission carries its own weight, and Allah is the Sustainer of you and your companions.' If these people do not support this truth, it is their own deprivation; God is not in need of them or their wealth.
'And do not extend your eyes toward that by which We have given enjoyment to partners of them' (Wa-la tamuddanna 'aynayka ila ma matta'na bihi azwajan minhum). 'Azwajan minhum' (partners of them) refers to those groups of Quraysh who held honor and power in Makkah and Ta'if etc. The Prophet extending his eyes toward their wealth and assets—Allah forbid—could not be because he had any greed for worldly wealth. Worldly wealth was ready to be sacrificed at his feet in the greatest amounts by the elites of Makkah and Ta'if, on the condition that he abandon his mission. But in response to their offer, he recited a few verses of faith to them. The Prophets and the righteous have never valued the wealth and assets of those who have not surrendered their hearts to their Lord. If a wise person sees a youth wasting his full youth and all his capabilities on a completely wrong target, he wishes: 'If only this short-sighted, foolish youth would value his youth and capabilities'; similarly, the Prophet also had this longing: 'If only the elites and the wealthy of my nation would recognize the value of those blessings that Allah has bestowed upon them, and let not their wealth and capabilities be wasted in the way of Satan.' This longing and regret relates entirely to the spirit of well-wishing and empathy. It contains absolutely no trace of greed or desire. But even this spirit of well-wishing has a limit. Upon reaching this limit, Allah stopped His Prophet from this.
'We have given enjoyment' (matta'na bihi) is an expression of a factual reality. In this world, if someone obtains wealth and assets, it is neither the inheritance of his ancestors nor the fruit of his intelligence and capability, but rather it is a gift of God, and Allah grants it to see: does this person, after purifying the wealth and resources given by God, become a grateful servant of God, or an ungrateful one? Foolish is that human who does not understand this fundamental reality and turns God's blessing into a cause of rebellion against God Himself.
'The flower of the worldly life' (zahrata al-hayati ad-dunya). In our view, it is a circumstantial clause (hal) to the pronoun in 'bihi'. Bringing a circumstantial clause to a genitive pronoun is well known in eloquent Arabic. We are presenting its examples in various places in this book. The meaning is: the wealth and assets with which We have blessed them has a transient splendor. If it has any benefit, it is that they may earn something for the Hereafter through it. If they do not do so, this splendor will blow away one day, and the darkness produced by it will remain forever.
'That We may test them therein' (li-naftinahum fihi). This refers to the divine practice (sunnah) which we have explained under the verse 'And We tried you with many trials'. Allah tests people in this world with trials and also with blessings. When He tests with trials, the objective is to test the servant's patience; and when He tests with blessings, the objective is to test his gratitude. It states: We have bestowed this wealth and assets upon them not because they were deserving of it, but rather to test what they make of our blessings. If they have understood the obligation of these blessings to mean that they should act arrogantly before Us upon receiving them, they will soon see the end of it!
'And the provision of your Lord is better and more enduring' (wa-rizqu Rabbika khayrun wa-abqa). Meaning, you do not need the patronage of these ungrateful and arrogant ones for your mission and your companions. What is to be granted to you and your companions from God is thousand times better and more enduring. What these proud ones possess has a transient splendor, but what God will bestow is eternal. It will never face decline... It must be kept in mind here that the plant of righteousness grows only from that water of life which is sent down by God for its nourishment. If you try to grow it with the water of every dirty pool, firstly, its growth from any impure water is against its nature, and even if it apparently grows, it will bear not the fruits of righteousness but the fruits of evil. This reality relates to the wisdom of the Qur'an and will be discussed in detail in its place. Here, limit yourself to this indication.

The Meaning of the Word 'Ahl'
The word 'ahl' here is not limited only to the meaning of the Prophet's household (Ahl al-Bayt). This word is used in a wide sense in Arabic—for example, Ahl al-Kitab (People of the Book), Ahl al-Injeel (People of the Gospel), Ahl al-Qaryah (People of the Town), Ahl Allah (People of Allah), Ahl al-Qurun (People of the Generations), and Ahl al-Hadith (People of Hadith) etc. In Lisan al-'Arab, it says: 'Ahl al-rajul are the people closest to him' (Ahl al-rajul refers to those people who have a special connection with that person); 'The ahl of every Prophet is his Ummah' (The ahl of a Prophet refers to his followers). There is a narration from Sayyiduna Abu Bakr al-Siddiq regarding appointing Hazrat Umar as caliph: when my Lord asks me on the Day of Resurrection: 'Whom have you appointed as governor over the Muslims?', I will reply: 'I have appointed as caliph over them the best of Your ahl' (ist'amaltu 'alayhim khayra ahlik). In the verse of Surah Maryam, it is said regarding Sayyiduna Ishmael: 'And he used to enjoin on his ahl prayer and zakat' (55); obviously, 'ahl' here does not refer only to Hazrat Ishmael's wife and children, but rather to all those people who were associated with him.
Similarly, in the verse under discussion, 'ahl' refers to all those people who believed in the Prophet (peace be upon him), whether they were his close relatives or other Muslims, and whether they were Qurayshi and Hashimi or maids and slaves—all of them have been described as the Prophet's 'ahl'. This is because, in reality, all those people who believe in the Prophet are members of his family, and the Prophet is concerned with looking after them just as someone is concerned with looking after his own close family. Reflect on how the word 'ahl' here has raised the status and honor of the poor Muslims! Those poor souls for whom there was no one to care, Allah included them in the family (ahl) of the Leader of the Universe (peace be upon him)!
'And enjoin prayer upon your ahl and be steadfast therein' (Wa'mur ahlaka bis-salati was-tabir 'alayha). It says: direct your people to perform prayer and remain firm on it. 'Prayer' refers to the same prayer commanded in the preceding verse. That is, to produce patience and steadfastness in yourself and your people, arrange the prayer, and maintain it with consistency. The correct meaning of 'was-tabir 'alayha' is: just as a farmer plows his field, sows seeds in it, waters it, and constantly monitors it, similarly you should perform full labor in this blessed field of yours and guard it; then see what blessings of religion and the world are obtained from it! Those blessings will be obtained that you will be delighted! You will not need to look toward anyone else!
'We do not ask you for provision' (La nas'aluka rizqan), meaning the responsibility of arranging provision for you and your devoted companions has neither been placed on you, nor do We demand it of you; its responsibility is upon Us. 'We provide for you' (nahnu narzuquka)—We are responsible for your provision; you do Our work and maintain the prayer. Leave the responsibility that is Ours to Us; do not be anxious about it. Sayyiduna Messiah clarified this reality thus: 'The laborer who is on duty is worthy of his wages.'
In the Islamic System, the Caliph and Ruler is the Guardian of the Islamic Family
Note also the singular second-person pronoun in 'narzuquka' (We provide for you). It does not say 'We provide for you and your people', but rather 'We provide for you'—this address to the Prophet is in his capacity as the head and guardian of the Islamic family. When this promise is made to the guardian of the family, it is obviously made for his entire family—i.e., all Muslims. In the Islamic system, the caliph and ruler holds the status of the guardian of the entire Islamic family, which is why he has to care for every single member of the family just as the head of a family cares for his wife and children. If I describe how Siddiq al-Akbar and Farooq al-A'zam maintained this reality during their respective caliphates, the discussion will drift to other aspects. In this era, many can be found mourning the grief of the masses on stages, but there is no one who, like Hazrat Umar, feels this responsibility: 'If even a hungry dog sleeps in the far corners of my state, the responsibility for it lies upon me'—and not only feel it, but prove their feeling through actions, to the point that this concern makes him negligent even of his own wife and children!
Prayer is the Guarantor of Provision. In the preceding phrase, there was a promise of worldly provision and grace. This is a glad tiding of success in the Hereafter: 'and the end is for piety' (wal-'aqibatu lit-taqwa), meaning the ultimate success is only for the people of piety; no one else has any share in it.
Here, it will seem highly strange to superficial observers how prayer can be a guarantor of a person's provision. No doubt, this reality cannot be explained to those who are neither familiar with the spirit of prayer nor have any acquaintance with a true observer of prayer. This thing is not to be explained, but rather to be known and experienced. Only experience can produce certainty of it. Those who are familiar with the ecstasy of prayer know what is in it. Do not consider my words as a boast. I am myself an inactive person, but I have seen with my own eyes in the pages of history and in walking humans such foreheads whose prostrations shamed the brilliance of the sun, and whose seeking of forgiveness was such that they rejected even the throne of Jamshed with their feet.

Verses 133-135
When the opponents were defeated on all sides in the field of arguments, their final weapon was to demand: 'If he is a Messenger, why does he not present a sign from his Lord in proof of his messengership?'—by sign, they meant some sensory miracle and also the kind of punishment with which the Qur'an was warning them in case of denying the Messenger. In raising this question, the People of the Book played a major part. Just as they taught the polytheists many questions in that era, of which examples have passed, they also taught this lesson: demand of this claimant of messengership that just as previous messengers showed many signs, to the point that punishments also befell their nations according to their news, why does he not show some sign of this sort? The Qur'an answered this demand, and in this answer, it kept in view both those who whispered doubts for these questions and those who presented them. In this verse, the first group is answered, and in the next verse, the second group.
The first group—i.e., the People of the Book—is answered: 'Is it not sufficient for them as a sign that the manifestation and confirmation of the glad tidings regarding the final Messenger in the previous scriptures has come before them, who confirmed and supported all those prophecies before them?' We have referenced these prophecies in detail in the commentaries of Surah Al-Baqarah and Al-Imran, and also clarified that describing the Prophet as 'musaddiq' (confirmer) of the previous scriptures means that his advent practically confirmed all those prophecies. This confirmation is expressed here by the word 'bayyinah' (clear proof).
Now, the second group—i.e., the polytheists of Arabia—is answered in view: these ill-fated people, influenced by the suggestions of others, demand punishment, but if We had destroyed them with a punishment before sending a messenger, they would have made this excuse on the Day of Resurrection: 'O our Lord, why did You not send a messenger to us so that we might have followed Your verses before we were humbled and disgraced?' Meaning: We sent Our Messenger to them to eliminate this excuse. Now the proof has been completed against them. If even after this they do not believe, then the very thing they are demanding will appear before them.
The words 'dhillah' (humiliation) and 'khizy' (disgrace) are used interchangeably in their general usage, but when they appear together, as they do here, a subtle difference arises between them. In this case, 'dhillah' refers to that humiliation which the one being humiliated feels within his own inner self, while 'khizy' refers to that disgrace which he faces before others. Meaning: we would neither have been humiliated in our own eyes nor would this turn of disgrace and public shame have occurred before others.
This is the final warning. In both preceding verses, the matter was expressed turning away in third-person pronouns. Here, to express the intensity of wrath, it is stated in the style of direct address: Say to them: now the decisive phase is before us. Both parties are waiting to see what the final outcome of this conflict will be and which way the wind blows. So wait for a while; soon it will become clear to you who were on the straight and even path and became those who reached the destination, and who were on the path of misguidance and fell into the pit of destruction!
In this verse, in my view, based on the clarity of context, the corresponding sentence of the comparison is deleted. Examples of such deletions have passed before. I have uncovered this deleted part.
With these lines, the commentary of the Surah is completed. May Allah forgive lapses and create space for the correct things.
